title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: table summarizes recommended measures variable type. PRE = Proportional Reduction Error. measures quantify much knowing one variable reduces prediction error . functions accept contingency table (class table, typically xtabs() table()).

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: assoc_measures() computes available measures : useful exploratory analysis. reporting, pick measure matches variable types.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) assoc_measures(tbl) #> Measure                            Estimate     SE  CI lower  CI upper      p  #> Cramer's V                            0.136     --     0.079     0.191  <.001  #> Contingency Coefficient               0.134     --        --        --  <.001  #> Lambda symmetric                      0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda R|C                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda C|R                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C             0.018  0.008     0.003     0.034   .023  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R             0.008  0.003     0.001     0.014   .022  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric     0.011  0.005     0.002     0.021   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C           0.018  0.008     0.003     0.032   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R           0.009  0.004     0.001     0.016   .021  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                -0.268  0.056    -0.378    -0.158  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                      -0.126  0.027    -0.180    -0.073  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                      -0.117  0.026    -0.167    -0.067  <.001  #> Somers' D R|C                        -0.091  0.020    -0.131    -0.052  <.001  #> Somers' D C|R                        -0.175  0.038    -0.249    -0.101  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Cramer’s V measures strength association two nominal variables. ranges 0 (association) 1 (perfect association). Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value. p-value tests null hypothesis association using Pearson chi-squared test.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) cramer_v(tbl) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: 2x2 tables, Phi equivalent Cramer’s V. spicy implements Phi \(\sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\), matching DescTools SPSS conventions – value always non-negative reports strength association, direction. recover signed direction, compute Pearson correlation two binary variables explicitly (e.g., cor(x, y) coding 0/1). p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) phi(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.006     0.000     0.063  .839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: contingency coefficient alternative Cramer’s V. upper bound depends table dimensions, makes harder compare across tables different sizes. p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: contingency_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.134        --        --  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: variables ordinal (ordered factors), measures account ordering appropriate Cramer’s V.

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Gamma ranges -1 +1. ignores tied pairs, makes sensitive direction association tends overestimate strength many ties. positive value means higher values one variable tend occur higher values . p-value tests H0: Gamma = 0 using Wald z-test.
code: tbl_ord <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) gamma_gk(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Tau-b adjusts ties ranges -1 +1. generally preferred Gamma square near-square tables. p-value tests H0: Tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_b(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.205     0.155     0.254  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Tau-c similar Tau-b adjusts rectangular tables number rows columns differ. p-value tests H0: Tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_c(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.200     0.151     0.248  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Somers’ D asymmetric measure: distinguishes dependent independent variable. default, row variable treated dependent (D(R|C)). p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: somers_d(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.208     0.157     0.258  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: measures answer specific question: much knowing column variable reduce error predicting row variable (vice versa)?

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Lambda measures proportional reduction classification error. can equal zero even variables associated, modal category change across columns. p-value tests H0: Lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) lambda_gk(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.012     0.000     0.039  .389

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Tau measures proportional reduction error predicting row variable column variable, using full distribution (just mode). p-value tests H0: Tau = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.017     0.008     0.026  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: uncertainty coefficient (Theil’s U) based entropy. measures much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty . p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: uncertainty_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.028     0.016     0.040  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: Yule’s Q defined 2x2 tables . ranges -1 +1 equivalent Gamma 2x2 tables. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) yule_q(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.015    -0.126     0.155  .839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: cross_tab() can automatically select appropriate measure via assoc_measure = "auto" (default). variables ordered factors, picks Kendall’s Tau-b; otherwise uses Cramer’s V. can override automatic choice:

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: functions support confidence intervals via detail = TRUE. confidence level defaults 95% can changed conf_level: get estimate p-value (CI), pass conf_level = NULL:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = 0.99) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.176     0.103     0.248  <.001 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #> Estimate      p #>    0.176  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures
text: detail = FALSE (default), functions return plain numeric scalar, R’s formatting rules apply. detail = TRUE, result uses custom print method defaults 3 decimal places. Pass digits change precision estimate, SE, CI columns. p-value follows APA-style formatting independent digits: 3 decimal places leading zero stripped (.045) <.001 threshold: digits argument works assoc_measures(): can also store result re-display different precision without recalculating:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, digits = 4) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>   0.1762    0.1203    0.2309  <.001 assoc_measures(tbl, digits = 2) #> Measure                            Estimate    SE  CI lower  CI upper      p  #> Cramer's V                             0.18    --      0.12      0.23  <.001  #> Contingency Coefficient                0.24    --        --        --  <.001  #> Lambda symmetric                       0.01  0.01      0.00      0.04   .389  #> Lambda R|C                             0.00  0.00      0.00      0.00     --  #> Lambda C|R                             0.02  0.03      0.00      0.07   .386  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C              0.02  0.00      0.01      0.03  <.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R              0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  <.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric      0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  <.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C            0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  <.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R            0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  <.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                  0.31  0.04      0.24      0.38  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                        0.20  0.03      0.16      0.25  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                        0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  <.001  #> Somers' D R|C                          0.21  0.03      0.16      0.26  <.001  #> Somers' D C|R                          0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  <.001 res <- cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) print(res, digits = 5) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>  0.17617   0.12031   0.23092  <.001

what: How a factor enters the model
title: Categorical predictors
text: R expands factor k levels k − 1 indicator (dummy) variables, one per non-reference level; remaining level becomes reference absorbed intercept (Gelman, Hill & Vehtari 2020, §10.4). table_regression() groups indicator rows parent variable renders reference level explicitly, (ref.) en dash statistic columns: coefficient comparison reference level, holding predictors constant — overall effect variable. Reading rows reference: unemployed respondents score average 3.6 points lower 0–100 wellbeing index employed respondents age (B = -3.60, 95% CI [-6.17, -1.03], p = .006); students inactive respondents data show clear difference employed (p = .441 .854). Nothing table compares students unemployed — contrast difference two coefficients, standard error printed . one specific non-reference contrast research question, cleanest route make one ends reference.
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + employment_status, data = sochealth) table_regression(fit) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable           │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)        │   67.38  1.62  [64.20, 70.56]  <.001  #>  age                │    0.04  0.03  [-0.02,  0.10]   .181  #>  employment_status: │                                       #>    Employed (ref.)  │     –     –          –          –     #>    Student          │    1.10  1.42  [-1.69,  3.88]   .441  #>    Unemployed       │   -3.60  1.31  [-6.17, -1.03]   .006  #>    Inactive         │    0.28  1.52  [-2.71,  3.27]   .854  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                  │ 1200                                  #>  R²                 │    0.01                               #>  Adj.R²             │    0.01                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

what: Choosing and changing the reference level
title: Categorical predictors
text: reference level presentation choice, modeling one: refitting another reference model reparameterized — identical fit, likelihood, AIC, fitted values; comparisons displayed change (Gelman, Hill & Vehtari 2020, §10.4). Every group now sits 3.6 4.7 points unemployed — fit read end. Two practical rules: let alphabetical order decide. factor() sorts levels alphabetically default, reference whichever category happens sort first — rarely one chosen, silent source confusing signs data come text files. Choose reference makes every displayed contrast readable: largest category (stable comparisons), unexposed / control category (epidemiology), substantively natural baseline. rare category makes poor reference — every contrast inherits noise.
code: d2 <- sochealth d2$employment_status <- relevel(d2$employment_status, ref = "Unemployed") fit_u <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + employment_status, data = d2) c(AIC(fit), AIC(fit_u)) #> [1] 10001.8 10001.8 table_regression(fit_u, keep = "employment_status") #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable            │    B      SE      95% CI       p    #> ─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  employment_status:  │                                     #>    Unemployed (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Employed          │    3.60  1.31  [1.03, 6.17]   .006  #>    Student           │    4.70  1.76  [1.25, 8.15]   .008  #>    Inactive          │    3.88  1.84  [0.27, 7.50]   .035  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                   │ 1200                                #>  R²                  │    0.01                             #>  Adj.R²              │    0.01                             #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

what: Does the factor matter at all? The joint test
title: Categorical predictors
text: per-level p-values answer “employment status matter?”: one tests single level reference, set changes reference choice. question factor whole joint test k − 1 coefficients — multiple-degree--freedom test, invariant reference (Harrell 2015, §2.6). Harrell’s rule run interpreting individual contrast: levels straddle significance, , joint test honest headline. change statistics nested comparison give exactly test — adding factor model lacks . One prerequisite: change statistics valid models fit rows, R’s listwise deletion silently drops different rows added variable carries missing values — code first restricts data cases complete every variable involved. table_regression() enforces : models different nobs refused, instructions refit common subset. F-change row joint 3-df test: F = 3.13, p = .025 — employment status improves model, driven unemployed contrast. glm fits, partial_chi2 column reports joint question per term likelihood-ratio chi-square df, without needing second model (see main vignette’s Term-level partial chi-square). rule cuts way : region, joint test gives F(5, 1193) = 0.89, p = .48 — evidence region matters (anova() two nested fits F-change test table reports). Fitting factor anyway scanning five per-level p-values “significant” contrast report alone multiplicity artifact — “overall test significant, can dangerous rely individual pairwise comparisons” (Harrell 2015, §2.6).
code: d_cc <- sochealth[complete.cases(   sochealth[, c("wellbeing_score", "age", "employment_status")] ), ] m0 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age, data = d_cc) m1 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + employment_status, data = d_cc) table_regression(list(m0, m1), nested = TRUE, show_columns = c("b", "p")) #> Hierarchical linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>                          Model 1          Model 2      #>                       ──────────────  ───────────────  #>  Variable           │    B       p       B        p    #> ────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)        │   67.00  <.001    67.38   <.001  #>  age                │    0.04   .177     0.04    .181  #>  employment_status: │                                  #>    Employed (ref.)  │                     –      –     #>    Student          │                    1.10    .441  #>    Unemployed       │                   -3.60    .006  #>    Inactive         │                    0.28    .854  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                  │ 1200            1200             #>  R²                 │    0.00            0.01          #>  Adj.R²             │    0.00            0.01          #>  ΔR²                │     –             +0.01          #>  F-change           │     –             +3.13          #>  p (change)         │     –               .025         #>  #> Note. Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). d_r <- sochealth[complete.cases(   sochealth[, c("wellbeing_score", "age", "region")] ), ] anova(lm(wellbeing_score ~ age,          data = d_r),       lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + region, data = d_r)) #> Analysis of Variance Table #>  #> Model 1: wellbeing_score ~ age #> Model 2: wellbeing_score ~ age + region #>   Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F) #> 1   1198 292107                            #> 2   1193 291017  5      1090 0.8936 0.4845

title: Categorical predictors
text: ordinal predictor — levels natural order, like self_rated_health (Poor < Fair < Good < good) — raises coding decision R quietly makes . ordered factor, R use dummy coding: uses orthogonal polynomial contrasts, table shows trend components instead level comparisons. spicy flags first time happens footer names coding: rows answer trend questions across ordered levels: .L (linear) dominant component — wellbeing rises steeply across health ratings — .Q (quadratic, B = -3.93, p < .001) says rise concave: steps get smaller toward top scale. .C (cubic, p = .235) adds nothing. decomposition legitimate reading — trend tests exactly polynomial contrasts — level-versus-reference reading regression tables intend. , convert plain factor (factor(x, ordered = FALSE)), message suggests vignettes collection .
code: fit_srh <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + self_rated_health,               data = sochealth) table_regression(fit_srh, show_columns = c("b", "se", "p")) #> Ordered factor(s) detected. Polynomial contrasts (the R default for `ordered()`) decompose the factor into orthogonal trend components: `.L` = linear, `.Q` = quadratic, `.C` = cubic, `^k` = degree k. Coefficients are trends across the ordered levels, NOT per-level effects against a reference. #> ℹ To display per-level (treatment) effects, refit with `factor(x, ordered = FALSE)` or set `options(contrasts = c("contr.treatment", "contr.treatment"))`. #> This message is displayed once per session. #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable           │    B      SE     p    #> ────────────────────┼────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)        │   60.57  1.34  <.001  #>  age                │    0.05  0.03   .042  #>  self_rated_health: │                       #>    .L               │   26.48  1.22  <.001  #>    .Q               │   -3.93  1.01  <.001  #>    .C               │   -0.89  0.75   .235  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                  │ 1180                  #>  R²                 │    0.35               #>  Adj.R²             │    0.34               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> Ordered factor `self_rated_health`: polynomial trends (.L = linear, .Q = quadratic, .C = cubic).

title: Categorical predictors
text: ordinal predictor poses substantive decision: enter numeric scores (1, 2, 3, … — one slope, read “per step scale”) dummies (k − 1 coefficients, order imposed)? scores model nested dummy model, likelihood-ratio test compares : fit codings let data say whether level coefficients depart linear trend scores (Agresti 2007, §4.4.3). nested = TRUE runs comparison. two examples land opposite sides decision. Case 1 — scores suffice. Education (3 levels) predicting smoking: Freeing education linear trend buys chi-square 0.03 1 df, p = .852 — evidence dummies improve scores — AIC agrees (1198.9 vs 1200.9). scores model simpler report (one coefficient: B = -0.45 per education step, log-odds scale) powerful genuine trend, spends one parameter dummies spend two. 3 levels test familiar counterpart: 1-df question Wald test .Q contrast ordered coding (z-test p = .852 ; GLM Wald likelihood-ratio answers agree asymptotically, exactly linear model) — quadratic component departure linearity. Case 2 — linearity rejected. Self-rated health (4 levels) predicting wellbeing: test rejects single slope: F-change = 12.96 2 df, p < .001. dummy coefficients show : steps Poor +14.97 (Fair), +28.01 (Good), +35.13 (good) — increments 15, 13, 7 points. effect flattens top scale, exactly concavity .Q contrast detected (2-df test identical joint test .Q .C). Forcing one slope 10.96 per step overstate last step understate first. Keep dummies. Three caveats keep decision honest (Harrell 2015, §§2.6 2.7.3; Grambsch & O’Brien 1991): non-significant test prove linearity — modest samples several codings fit adequately, integer scores assume steps equally spaced, substantive claim scale, statistical default. Test--simplify inflates error. Reporting scores model chosen advance makes subsequent test’s distribution wrong. Grambsch O’Brien showed, quadratic-versus-linear case, pre-specified 2-df joint test remains close optimal even truth linear; logic carries factor’s full-df test — factor control variable rather focus, dummies cost little assume nothing. stakes rise number levels. handful levels — three five, Harrell’s account — variable usually modeled polytomous factor: dummies spend parameter two assume nothing. advantage scores grows k larger model dense relative sample.
code: sh <- sochealth sh$education  <- factor(sh$education, ordered = FALSE) sh$educ_score <- as.numeric(sh$education) sh <- sh[complete.cases(sh[, c("smoking", "sex", "age", "education")]), ]  g_lin <- glm(smoking ~ sex + age + educ_score, data = sh,              family = binomial) g_fac <- glm(smoking ~ sex + age + education,  data = sh,              family = binomial) table_regression(list(g_lin, g_fac), nested = TRUE,                  show_columns = c("b", "p")) #> Hierarchical logistic regression: smoking #>  #>                                Model 1          Model 2      #>                             ──────────────  ───────────────  #>  Variable                 │    B       p       B        p    #> ──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │   -0.67   .036    -1.11   <.001  #>  sex:                     │                                  #>    Female (ref.)          │     –     –         –      –     #>    Male                   │   -0.04   .805    -0.04    .800  #>  age                      │    0.01   .212     0.01    .214  #>  educ_score               │   -0.45  <.001                   #>  education:               │                                  #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │                     –      –     #>    Upper secondary        │                   -0.48    .005  #>    Tertiary               │                   -0.91   <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                        │ 1175            1175             #>  R² (McFadden)            │    0.02            0.02          #>  R² (Nagelkerke)          │    0.03            0.03          #>  AIC                      │ 1198.9          1200.9           #>  Δχ²                      │     –             +0.03          #>  p (change)               │     –               .852         #>  #> Note. Logistic regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (Fisher information). d3 <- sochealth[complete.cases(   sochealth[, c("wellbeing_score", "age", "self_rated_health")] ), ] d3$srh_score  <- as.numeric(d3$self_rated_health) d3$srh_factor <- factor(d3$self_rated_health, ordered = FALSE)  s_lin <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + srh_score,  data = d3) s_fac <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + srh_factor, data = d3) table_regression(list(s_lin, s_fac), nested = TRUE,                  show_columns = c("b", "p")) #> Hierarchical linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>                     Model 1          Model 2      #>                  ──────────────  ───────────────  #>  Variable      │    B       p       B        p    #> ───────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)   │   34.41  <.001    41.04   <.001  #>  age           │    0.05   .043     0.05    .042  #>  srh_score     │   10.96  <.001                   #>  srh_factor:   │                                  #>    Poor (ref.) │                     –      –     #>    Fair        │                   14.97   <.001  #>    Good        │                   28.01   <.001  #>    Very good   │                   35.13   <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │ 1180            1180             #>  R²            │    0.33            0.35          #>  Adj.R²        │    0.33            0.34          #>  ΔR²           │     –             +0.01          #>  F-change      │     –            +12.96          #>  p (change)    │     –              <.001         #>  #> Note. Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

title: Categorical predictors
text: Dummy coding answers “far level reference”; scores answer “much per step, forced equal”. third question — “big step?” — coding: MASS’s successive-difference contrasts, whose coefficients differences adjacent levels (Venables & Ripley 2002). Harrell (2015, §2.7.3) reaches reading threshold indicators [X ≥ level]; two parameterizations give identical fits. row one step scale: Fair sits 14.97 points Poor, Good 13.04 Fair, good 7.12 Good — flattening gradient read directly, standard error per step. natural display steps finding. (Two notes: non-treatment contrasts reference level, rows render flat rather grouped factor (ref.) row; joint test factor unchanged — model, different questions asked .)
code: d3$srh_sdif <- d3$self_rated_health contrasts(d3$srh_sdif) <- MASS::contr.sdif(4) table_regression(   lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + srh_sdif, data = d3),   keep = "srh_sdif" ) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable    │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  srh_sdif2-1 │   14.97  1.80  [11.44, 18.51]  <.001  #>  srh_sdif3-2 │   13.04  0.95  [11.18, 14.89]  <.001  #>  srh_sdif4-3 │    7.12  0.91  [ 5.33,  8.91]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n           │ 1180                                  #>  R²          │    0.35                               #>  Adj.R²      │    0.34                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

what: The reverse temptation: categorizing a continuous predictor
title: Categorical predictors
text: Turning categorical variable number sometimes justified (scores ); turning continuous predictor categories almost never (Harrell 2015, §2.4.1). sochealth ships bmi conventional bmi_category (normal weight / overweight / obesity), cost can shown directly: continuous fit explains twice variance half parameters (R² = .020 1 df .010 2 df; AIC 9884.2 vs 9898.1). Harrell’s argument one table: categorization discards variation within intervals — every BMI 25.0 29.9 treated exposure — assumes wellbeing jumps cut-points flat , far stronger assumption linearity. estimates also stop portable: “Overweight vs Normal weight” coefficient (B = -1.69, p = .082) depends sample sits inside 25.0–29.9 interval, per-unit slope (B = -0.60 points per BMI unit, p < .001) . nonlinear effect worry, remedy flexible continuous fit — polynomial spline — bins: quadratic term adds nothing (AIC 9885.9 9884.2 linear fit), linear fit stands. Cut-points chosen looking outcome damage still: p-values CIs “optimal” cut-points invalid without special corrections, cut-points fail replicate across studies (Harrell 2015, §2.4.1). Categories remain fine descriptive tables presentation-layer grouping — mistake putting model continuous measurement exists.
code: d4 <- sochealth[complete.cases(   sochealth[, c("wellbeing_score", "bmi", "bmi_category")] ), ] d4$bmi_category <- factor(d4$bmi_category, ordered = FALSE)  # per-level dummies, as above b_cont <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ bmi,          data = d4) b_cat  <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ bmi_category, data = d4) table_regression(list(b_cont, b_cat), show_columns = c("b", "p"),                  show_fit_stats = c("nobs", "r2", "AIC")) #> Linear regression comparison: wellbeing_score #>  #>                              Model 1         Model 2      #>                           ──────────────  ──────────────  #>  Variable               │    B       p       B       p    #> ────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)            │   84.48  <.001    70.47  <.001  #>  bmi                    │   -0.60  <.001                  #>  bmi_category:          │                                 #>    Normal weight (ref.) │                     –     –     #>    Overweight           │                   -1.69   .082  #>    Obesity              │                   -5.04  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                      │ 1188            1188            #>  R²                     │    0.02            0.01         #>  AIC                    │ 9884.2          9898.1          #>  #> Note. Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). AIC(b_cont, update(b_cont, . ~ . + I(bmi^2))) #>                                  df      AIC #> b_cont                            3 9884.236 #> update(b_cont, . ~ . + I(bmi^2))  4 9885.893

Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

title: Categorical predictors
text: vignette("table-regression", package = "spicy") shared table mechanics (variance estimators, nested layouts, filtering, output formats). vignette("table-regression-ordinal", package = "spicy") ordinal outcomes — proportional-odds model, tests relaxations. vignette("table-regression-multinomial", package = "spicy") scores-vs-dummies decision inside multinomial model, freed coding costs one parameter per equation.

title: Frequency tables and cross-tabulations
text: Weights supplied via weights, just like freq(). default differs, however: cross_tab() defaults rescale = FALSE (raw weighted counts), whereas freq() defaults rescale = TRUE. Without rescaling, table shows raw weighted counts: Pass rescale = TRUE make weighted total match unweighted sample size:
code: cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight) #> Crosstable: smoking x education (N) #>  #>  Values   │   Lower secondary    Upper secondary    Tertiary │   Total  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  No       │               176                417         324 │     917  #>  Yes      │                79                114          60 │     253  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  Total    │               255                531         384 │    1170  #>  #> Chi-2(2) = 21.3, p <.001 #> Cramer's V = 0.13 #> Weight: weight cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight, rescale = TRUE) #> Crosstable: smoking x education (N) #>  #>  Values   │   Lower secondary    Upper secondary    Tertiary │   Total  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  No       │               176                419         325 │     921  #>  Yes      │                79                115          60 │     254  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  Total    │               255                534         385 │    1175  #>  #> Chi-2(2) = 21.4, p <.001 #> Cramer's V = 0.13 #> Weight: weight (rescaled)

title: Getting started with spicy
text: varlist() (shortcut vl()) gives compact overview every variable data frame: name, label, representative values, class, number distinct values, valid observations, missing values. title: Getting started with spicy
text: quick overview available association statistics, pass contingency table assoc_measures(): Individual functions cramer_v(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b() return scalar default. Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value:
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) assoc_measures(tbl) #> Measure                            Estimate     SE  CI lower  CI upper      p  #> Cramer's V                            0.136     --     0.079     0.191  <.001  #> Contingency Coefficient               0.134     --        --        --  <.001  #> Lambda symmetric                      0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda R|C                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda C|R                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C             0.018  0.008     0.003     0.034   .023  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R             0.008  0.003     0.001     0.014   .022  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric     0.011  0.005     0.002     0.021   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C           0.018  0.008     0.003     0.032   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R           0.009  0.004     0.001     0.016   .021  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                -0.268  0.056    -0.378    -0.158  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                      -0.126  0.027    -0.180    -0.073  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                      -0.117  0.026    -0.167    -0.067  <.001  #> Somers' D R|C                        -0.091  0.020    -0.131    -0.052  <.001  #> Somers' D C|R                        -0.175  0.038    -0.249    -0.101  <.001 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  <.001

Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3). fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking, data = sochealth) table_regression(fit) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.66  [61.95, 68.45]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.03  [-0.01,  0.11]   .130  #>  sex:            │                                       #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.91  [ 2.08,  5.63]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                       #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.11  [-3.89,  0.45]   .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                  #>  R²              │    0.02                               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

title: Getting started with spicy
text: mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n() compute row-wise statistics across selected columns, automatic handling missing values.
code: sochealth |>   dplyr::mutate(     mean_sat  = mean_n(select = starts_with("life_sat")),     sum_sat   = sum_n(select = starts_with("life_sat"), min_valid = 2),     n_missing = count_n(select = starts_with("life_sat"), special = "NA")   ) |>   dplyr::select(starts_with("life_sat"), mean_sat, sum_sat, n_missing) |>   head() |>   as.data.frame() #>   life_sat_health life_sat_work life_sat_relationships life_sat_standard #> 1               5             3                      5                 5 #> 2               4             4                      5                 5 #> 3               3             2                      5                 3 #> 4               3             4                      3                 2 #> 5               4             5                      4                 4 #> 6               5             5                      5                 3 #>   mean_sat sum_sat n_missing #> 1     4.50      18         0 #> 2     4.50      18         0 #> 3     3.25      13         0 #> 4     3.00      12         0 #> 5     4.25      17         0 #> 6     4.50      18         0

title: Getting started with spicy
text: See ?varlist inspect variables, labels, values, missing data. See ?freq one-way frequency tables (weights, sorting, custom missing values, labelled-data display modes). See ?cross_tab full list arguments (weights, simulation, association measures). See ?assoc_measures complete list association statistics; ?cramer_v canonical entry point. See ?table_categorical grouped one-way categorical tables. See ?table_continuous continuous summaries group comparisons. See ?table_continuous_lm model-based mean-comparison tables robust / cluster-robust / bootstrap / jackknife SE, case weights, additive covariate adjustment. See ?table_regression lm / glm coefficient tables APA-aligned formatting, robust variance, standardised coefficients, average marginal effects, hierarchical comparisons, side--side multi-model layouts. See ?mean_n, ?sum_n, ?count_n row-wise summaries optional minimum-valid-values rules. See ?code_book generate interactive HTML codebook; ?label_from_names derive variable labels "code. label"-style column names (e.g., LimeSurvey exports).

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: Use function matches unit want report: practice, follow APA sequence: start table_categorical() smoking, education, activity — APA Table 1 categorical descriptors; use table_continuous() BMI, well-, income — Table 1 continuous descriptors Table 2 unadjusted group comparisons; switch table_continuous_lm() comparison must account survey weights, robust SE, covariate adjustment; finish table_regression() substantive model fitted — APA Table 3 predictors, factor groupings, reference rows, (optionally) standardised coefficients, marginal effects, nested model comparisons. first three functions live inside select / data-frame grammar; table_regression() fit-first — build model usual R way (lm() glm()) hand object . four share post-construction grammar (output, labels, digits, decimal_mark, align), swapping functions never breaks rendering pipeline.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: three descriptive functions share core arguments: argument pattern used three cases: select chooses reported variables; defines grouping structure; labels cleans row labels; output decides result rendered exported. model-based continuous tables, pattern applies, must single predictor one linear model fit per outcome. table_regression() joins labels / output / decimal_mark / digits grammar fit-first: rather expressing model structure inline select , pass one several already-fitted lm() glm() objects: Linear regression: wellbeing_score split intentional. descriptive trio (categorical, continuous, continuous_lm) reports data — select describe want see. table_regression() reports model — model formula already declared predictors, interactions, polynomials, transformations, splines, contrasts report, passing select / duplicate model object’s information risk diverging .
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   labels = c(     bmi = "Body mass index",     wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",     life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"   ),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   weights = weight ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index                │        25.96                23.39         #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │        67.55                80.88         #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │         3.45                 4.39         #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index                │    26.16      <.001  0.13  1188  #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │    66.52      <.001  0.19  1200  #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │     3.39      <.001  0.15  1192 fit <- lm(   wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + physical_activity,   data = sochealth ) table_regression(   fit,   labels = c(     age               = "Age (years)",     sex               = "Sex",     smoking           = "Smoking status",     physical_activity = "Regular physical activity"   ),   output = "tinytable" )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text:
#fxwnvjxbbl table { font-family: system-ui, ‘Segoe UI’, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, ‘Apple Color Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Symbol’, ‘Noto Color Emoji’; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale; }

#fxwnvjxbbl thead, #fxwnvjxbbl tbody, #fxwnvjxbbl tfoot, #fxwnvjxbbl tr, #fxwnvjxbbl td, #fxwnvjxbbl th { border-style: none; }

#fxwnvjxbbl p { margin: 0; padding: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_table { display: table; border-collapse: collapse; line-height: normal; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; color: #333333; font-size: 16px; font-weight: normal; font-style: normal; background-color: #FFFFFF; width: auto; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #A8A8A8; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #A8A8A8; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_caption { padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_title { color: #333333; font-size: 125%; font-weight: initial; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-width: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_subtitle { color: #333333; font-size: 85%; font-weight: initial; padding-top: 3px; padding-bottom: 5px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-top-color: #FFFFFF; border-top-width: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_heading { background-color: #FFFFFF; text-align: center; border-bottom-color: #FFFFFF; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_bottom_border { border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_col_headings { border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_col_heading { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; text-transform: inherit; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: bottom; padding-top: 5px; padding-bottom: 6px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; overflow-x: hidden; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_column_spanner_outer { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 0; padding-bottom: 0; padding-left: 4px; padding-right: 4px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_column_spanner_outer:first-child { padding-left: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_column_spanner_outer:last-child { padding-right: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_column_spanner { border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: bottom; padding-top: 5px; padding-bottom: 5px; overflow-x: hidden; display: inline-block; width: 100%; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_spanner_row { border-bottom-style: hidden; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_group_heading { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 2px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; text-align: left; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_empty_group_heading { padding: 0.5px; color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 2px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_from_md > :first-child { margin-top: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_from_md > :last-child { margin-bottom: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; margin: 10px; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 1px; border-top-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; overflow-x: hidden; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_stub { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-right-style: solid; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_stub_row_group { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-right-style: solid; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; vertical-align: top; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_row_group_first td { border-top-width: 2px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_row_group_first th { border-top-width: 2px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_summary_row { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_first_summary_row { border-top-style: solid; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_first_summary_row.thick { border-top-width: 2px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_last_summary_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_grand_summary_row { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_first_grand_summary_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-top-style: double; border-top-width: 6px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_last_grand_summary_row_top { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-style: double; border-bottom-width: 6px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_striped { background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_table_body { border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_footnotes { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-style: none; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_footnote { margin: 0px; font-size: 90%; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_sourcenotes { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-style: none; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_sourcenote { font-size: 90%; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_left { text-align: left; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_center { text-align: center; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_right { text-align: right; font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_font_normal { font-weight: normal; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_font_bold { font-weight: bold; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_font_italic { font-style: italic; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_super { font-size: 65%; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_footnote_marks { font-size: 75%; vertical-align: 0.4em; position: initial; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_asterisk { font-size: 100%; vertical-align: 0; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_indent_1 { text-indent: 5px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_indent_2 { text-indent: 10px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_indent_3 { text-indent: 15px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_indent_4 { text-indent: 20px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .gt_indent_5 { text-indent: 25px; }

#fxwnvjxbbl .katex-display { display: inline-flex !important; margin-bottom: 0.75em !important; }

#fxwnvjxbbl div.Reactable > div.rt-table > div.rt-thead > div.rt-tr.rt-tr-group-header > div.rt-th-group:{ height: 0px !important; }

.gt_table thead tr:first-child { border-top: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table thead tr.gt_spanner_row { border-bottom-style: none !important; }

.gt_table thead th, .gt_table thead td { background-color: transparent !important; }

.gt_table thead tr:last-child th[id=“LL”], .gt_table thead tr:last-child th[id=“UL”] { border-top: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table thead tr:last-child { border-bottom: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table tbody tr:last-child { border-bottom: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table tbody tr { border-top-style: none !important; border-bottom-style: none !important; }

.gt_table .gt_col_heading, .gt_table .gt_spanner { white-space: nowrap !important; }

.gt_table .gt_row .gt_right, .gt_table .gt_row .gt_center { white-space: nowrap !important; }

.gt_table, .gt_heading, .gt_col_headings, .gt_col_heading, .gt_column_spanner_outer, .gt_column_spanner, .gt_title, .gt_subtitle, .gt_sourcenotes, .gt_sourcenote { background-color: transparent !important; color: currentColor !important; } better summary-table path article already organized around simple linear models, weighted analyses, robust standard errors.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = sex,     vcov = "HC3",     statistic = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )
