.gt_table, .gt_heading, .gt_col_headings, .gt_col_heading, .gt_column_spanner_outer, .gt_column_spanner, .gt_title, .gt_subtitle, .gt_sourcenotes, .gt_sourcenote { background-color: transparent !important; color: currentColor !important; } Average marginal effects (ame) useful next odds ratio report probability-scale change predictor — quantity reviewers want interpret directly.
code: fit <- lm(   wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + physical_activity,   data = sochealth ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_regression(     fit,     standardized = "refit",     show_columns = c("b", "beta", "ci", "p"),     vcov = "HC3",     output = "gt"   ) ) fit_unadj <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ smoking, data = sochealth) fit_adj   <- lm(   wellbeing_score ~ smoking + age + sex + physical_activity,   data = sochealth ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_regression(     list("Unadjusted" = fit_unadj, "Adjusted" = fit_adj),     show_columns = c("b", "ci", "p"),     output = "gt"   ) ) fit_glm <- glm(   smoking ~ age + sex + physical_activity,   data = sochealth,   family = binomial() ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_regression(     fit_glm,     exponentiate = TRUE,     show_columns = c("b", "ci", "p", "ame", "ame_ci"),     output = "gt"   ) ) #> Warning: `"ame"` and `"p"` shown without `"ame_p"`: the `p` column is for B (or beta), not the AME. They can differ under non-linear links or interactions. #> ℹ Add `"ame_p"` to display the AME-specific p-value.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: four functions support reporting formats: Pick output based table going, analysis . underlying selection grouping pattern stays . want object fits naturally Word PowerPoint workflows can also rendered HTML documents, flextable good choice:
code: if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   ) }

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: four summary-table helpers return regular gt, tinytable, flextable objects, can keep styling native package API. includes table_regression(): nothing fit-first interface changes rendering engine produces. Use gt:: functions want keep gt workflow: Use tinytable:: functions want lightweight table-specific styling: Use flextable:: functions want keep working toward Office HTML document output. example shown code dark pkgdown theme reliable preview final flextable HTML rendering:
code: tab <- pkgdown_dark_gt(table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "gt" ))  tab |>   gt::tab_header(     title = "Health behaviors by education",     subtitle = "Categorical summary table"   ) |>   gt::tab_source_note(     gt::md("*Percentages are computed within each education group.*")   ) tab <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" )  tab |>   tinytable::style_tt(     i = 2:3,     j = 2:5,     background = "red",     color = "white",     bold = TRUE   ) if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   tab <- table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   )    tab |>     flextable::theme_booktabs() |>     flextable::autofit() |>     flextable::fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") }

what: Keep the detailed options in the function-specific articles
title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: dedicated articles go deeper function: table_categorical() covers missing values, level filtering, association measures, one-way frequency-style tables. table_continuous() covers grouped descriptive statistics, parametric nonparametric tests, effect sizes. table_continuous_lm() covers estimated marginal means slopes linear models, robust / cluster-robust / bootstrap / jackknife variance, case weights, additive covariate adjustment (G-computation equal-weight), four effect-size families noncentral CIs. table_regression() covers single- multi-model coefficient tables lm / glm, four standardisation methods, partial effect sizes noncentral-F CIs, average marginal effects, hierarchical (nested = TRUE) comparisons, multiplicity correction, response-scale reporting GLMs. Use vignette final reporting overview, consult function-specific articles need detailed controls.

title: Categorical summary tables
text: grouped tables, provide data frame, one selected variables, grouping variable: default output "default", prints styled ASCII table console. Use output = "data.frame" get plain numeric data frame suitable processing.

title: Categorical summary tables
text: Omit build frequency-style table selected variables:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   output = "default" ) #> Categorical table #>  #>  Variable            │   n      %     #> ─────────────────────┼─────────────── #>  smoking             │                #>    No                │  926    77.2   #>    Yes               │  249    20.8   #>    (Missing)         │   25     2.1   #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity   │                #>    No                │  650    54.2   #>    Yes               │  550    45.8

title: Categorical summary tables
text: table_categorical() supports several output formats. table summarizes options:

title: Categorical summary tables
text: "gt" format produces table APA-style borders, column spanners, proper alignment:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),     by = education,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex,   output = "tinytable" )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: Use output = "data.frame" wide numeric data frame (one row per modality), output = "long" long format (one row per modality x group):
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable     Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking        No               179              68.6               415 #> 2  smoking       Yes                78              29.9               112 #> 3  smoking (Missing)                 4               1.5                12 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              77.0        332       83.0     926    77.2 2.012877e-05 #> 2              20.8         59       14.8     249    20.8 2.012877e-05 #> 3               2.2          9        2.2      25     2.1 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V #> 1  0.1356677 #> 2  0.1356677 #> 3  0.1356677

title: Categorical summary tables
text: default, table_categorical() uses variable names row headers. Use labels argument provide human-readable labels. Two forms accepted (matching table_continuous() table_continuous_lm()): named character vector keyed column name data – recommended form. listed columns relabelled; others fall back column name. positional character vector length select – legacy spicy < 0.11.0 form, kept backward compatibility.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     labels = c(       smoking           = "Smoking status",       physical_activity = "Regular physical activity"     ),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: table_categorical() picks association measure per row variable based variable type (assoc_measure = "auto", default): 2x2 (binary row variable vs. binary ) -> phi, ordered factors -> Kendall’s tau_b, otherwise -> Cramer’s V. chosen measures differ across rows, column header collapses "Effect size" APA-style Note. line documents measure used variable. Override single string uniform application, named vector mix measures per row: Add confidence intervals assoc_ci = TRUE. rendered formats (gt, tinytable, flextable, word), CI shown inline: data formats ("data.frame", "long", "excel", "clipboard"), separate CI lower CI upper columns added:
code: # Uniform: same measure for every row variable table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_measure = "lambda",   output = "tinytable" ) # Per-row: pick the right measure for each variable. # `smoking` x `education` is 2x3 (binary x ordered) -> Cramer's V; # `self_rated_health` x `education` is ordered x ordered -> Tau-b. # The mixed result collapses the header to "Effect size" and adds an # APA `Note.` line documenting the per-row measure. table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, self_rated_health),   by = education,   assoc_measure = c(     smoking           = "cramer_v",     self_rated_health = "tau_b"   ),   output = "tinytable" ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     assoc_ci = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable     Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking        No               179              68.6               415 #> 2  smoking       Yes                78              29.9               112 #> 3  smoking (Missing)                 4               1.5                12 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              77.0        332       83.0     926    77.2 2.012877e-05 #> 2              20.8         59       14.8     249    20.8 2.012877e-05 #> 3               2.2          9        2.2      25     2.1 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V   CI lower  CI upper #> 1  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716 #> 2  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716 #> 3  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716

title: Categorical summary tables
text: Pass survey weights weights argument. Use rescale = TRUE total weighted N matches unweighted N:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     weights = "weight",     rescale = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: default, rows missing values dropped (drop_na = TRUE). Set drop_na = FALSE display “(Missing)” category:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: Use levels_keep display specific modalities. order specify controls display order, useful placing “(Missing)” first highlight missingness:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     levels_keep = c("(Missing)", "Low", "High"),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: Control number digits percentages, p-values, association measure: p_digits drives displayed precision p column small-p threshold (p_digits = 3 -> <.001, p_digits = 4 -> <.0001), matching table_continuous() table_continuous_lm().
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = smoking,     by = education,     percent_digits = 2,     p_digits = 4,     v_digits = 3,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: default (align = "decimal") numeric columns aligned decimal mark, standard scientific-publication convention used SPSS, SAS, LaTeX siunitx, native primitives gt::cols_align_decimal() tinytable::style_tt(align = "d"). Engines without native primitive (flextable, word, clipboard, ASCII print) get alignment via leading / trailing space padding, centring default body font — single-font policy table_regression(). "center" "right" apply literal alignment:

title: Categorical summary tables
text: table_categorical() returns object can coerced plain data.frame / tbl_df (stripping spicy formatting attributes) piped broom::tidy() / broom::glance() downstream tidyverse-stats workflow:
code: out <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex ) #> Categorical table by sex #>  #>  Variable          │ Female n  Female %  Male n  Male %  Total n  Total %   p    #> ───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  smoking           │                                                       .713  #>    No              │   475       76.6     451     77.8     926     77.2          #>    Yes             │   131       21.1     118     20.3     249     20.8          #>    (Missing)       │    14        2.3      11      1.9      25      2.1          #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity │                                                       .832  #>    No              │   334       53.9     316     54.5     650     54.2          #>    Yes             │   286       46.1     264     45.5     550     45.8          #>  #>  Variable          │ Phi  #> ───────────────────┼───── #>  smoking           │ .01  #>    No              │      #>    Yes             │      #>    (Missing)       │      #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity │ .01  #>    No              │      #>    Yes             │  # One row per (variable x level x group) with broom-style columns # (outcome, level, group, n, proportion). The synthetic Total # margin is excluded so each observation is counted once. broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>    outcome           level     group      n proportion #>    <chr>             <chr>     <chr>  <int>      <dbl> #>  1 smoking           No        Female   475      0.766 #>  2 smoking           No        Male     451      0.778 #>  3 smoking           Yes       Female   131      0.211 #>  4 smoking           Yes       Male     118      0.203 #>  5 smoking           (Missing) Female    14      0.023 #>  6 smoking           (Missing) Male      11      0.019 #>  7 physical_activity No        Female   334      0.539 #>  8 physical_activity No        Male     316      0.545 #>  9 physical_activity Yes       Female   286      0.461 #> 10 physical_activity Yes       Male     264      0.455  # One row per outcome with the omnibus chi-squared test and the # chosen association measure (test_type, statistic, df, p.value, # assoc_type, assoc_value, assoc_ci_lower / assoc_ci_upper, n_total). broom::glance(out) #> # A tibble: 2 × 10 #>   outcome           test_type   statistic    df p.value assoc_type assoc_value #>   <chr>             <chr>           <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>            <dbl> #> 1 physical_activity chi_squared    0.0452     1   0.832 Phi            0.00614 #> 2 smoking           chi_squared    0.136      1   0.713 Phi            0.0107  #> # ℹ 3 more variables: assoc_ci_lower <dbl>, assoc_ci_upper <dbl>, n_total <int>

what: Exporting to Excel, Word, or clipboard
title: Categorical summary tables
text: Excel export, provide file path: Word, use output = "word": can also copy directly clipboard pasting spreadsheet text editor:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "excel",   excel_path = "my_table.xlsx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "word",   word_path = "my_table.docx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   output = "clipboard" )

title: Categorical summary tables
text: See vignette("table-continuous", package = "spicy") continuous counterpart classical group-comparison tests. See vignette("table-continuous-lm", package = "spicy") model-based continuous summary tables. See vignette("table-regression", package = "spicy") full coefficient table one several fitted lm / glm models (APA Table 3). See vignette("summary-tables-reporting", package = "spicy") cross-function reporting workflow ties four summary-table helpers together along APA Table 1 / 2 / 3 sequence.

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Use select one continuous outcomes single predictor: categorical predictors, table reports estimated means level. predictor dichotomous, can also show single mean difference confidence interval.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi, life_sat_health),   by = sex ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index                │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │    3.51       3.59          0.08         #>  #>  Variable                       │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │    2.13      5.64     <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index                │    0.09      0.93      .018  0.00  1188  #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │   -0.06      0.22      .267  0.00  1192

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Use vcov = "HC*" want heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors tests: HC* family computed via [sandwich::vcovHC()] includes "HC0", "HC1", "HC2", "HC3" (default small / moderate samples), "HC4", "HC4m", "HC5".
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   vcov = "HC3",   statistic = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL   t      p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.12       5.65     4.32  <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93     2.38   .018  0.00  1188  #>  #> Note. Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: observations independent (repeated measurements per individual, students nested classes, panel data), classical HC* standard errors biased downward. Use CR* family together cluster = id_var get cluster-robust inference, dispatched clubSandwich: "CR2" recommended default (Bell & McCaffrey 2002; Pustejovsky & Tipton 2018). produces fractional Satterthwaite degrees freedom, rendered displayed test header e.g. t(8.7) F(2, 12.4). "CR1" matches Stata’s vce(cluster id) default.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sleep,   select = extra,   by = group,   vcov = "CR2",   cluster = ID,   statistic = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by group #>  #>  Variable │ M (1)  M (2)  Δ (2 - 1)  95% CI LL  95% CI UL  t(9)   p     R²   n   #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  extra    │ 0.75   2.33     1.58       0.70       2.46     4.06  .003  0.16  20  #>  #> Note. Std. errors: cluster-robust (CR2), clusters by ID.

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: situations residual distribution non-standard sample small, vcov = "bootstrap" vcov = "jackknife" provide resampling-based variance estimators pure base R (dependency added): cluster supplied, bootstrap switches cluster bootstrap (Cameron, Gelbach & Miller 2008) jackknife leave-one-cluster-(Quenouille 1956). estimators use asymptotic inference: z single-coefficient contrasts chi^2(q) global Wald test k > 2 categorical predictors, rendered displayed test header.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = sex,   vcov = "bootstrap",   boot_n = 1000  # default )

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Use weights want weighted estimated means slopes model-based table: often natural summary-table function reporting workflow already relies weighted linear models.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   weights = weight,   show_weighted_n = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.55                80.88         #>  Body mass index               │        25.96                23.39         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    n    Weighted n  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.52      <.001  0.19  1200   1196.47    #>  Body mass index               │    26.16      <.001  0.13  1188   1183.32

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: numeric, table_continuous_lm() reports slope rather group means: need underlying returned data processing, use output = "data.frame" wide raw table output = "long" analytic long table.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                      │  B    95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.04    -0.02      0.10      .176  0.00  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.04     0.02      0.05     <.001  0.02  1188  #>  #> Note. Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: comparison -group means potentially confounded variables, supply covariates. per-outcome linear model becomes lm(outcome ~ + covariate1 + covariate2 + ...), reported Δ (categorical ) slope (numeric ), CI, p, R² effect size adjusted covariates. default ASCII print emits APA-style footer naming adjustment reader knows estimand reading. covariates accepts full tidyselect vocabulary (c(age, sex), tidyselect::starts_with("life_sat_"), tidyselect::all_of(cov_names)) literal character vector. covariate also appears select silently auto-excluded outcome list (mirroring / weights excluded select); covariate equal rejected front.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.27      70.93          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.65      26.23          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.08       5.23     <.001  0.22  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (proportional). #> Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3).

what: Two estimands for the adjusted marginal means
title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Two methodologically distinct conventions exist computing covariate-adjusted per-group means displayed table (M (level) columns printed output, emmean / emmean_se / emmean_ci_lower / emmean_ci_upper columns output = "long" data frame) categorical. table_continuous_lm() exposes choice via adjustment; valid produce different numerical answers least one factor covariate non-uniform observed proportions. adjustment = "proportional" (default) — G-computation / population-weighted standardisation. focal level , predictions evaluated every observation data replaced level (covariates kept observed values), averaged. convention Stata’s margins marginaleffects::avg_predictions(variables). Interpretation: “predicted mean population everyone = lvl, holding actual covariate profile?”. Best causal / counterfactual reporting. adjustment = "balanced" — equal-weight averaging synthetic grid. Factor-covariate level combinations crossed weighted uniformly (1 / k); numeric covariates fixed sample mean. convention emmeans::emmeans() (default), SPSS UNIANOVA EMMEANS, SAS LSMEANS. Interpretation: “predicted mean assuming balanced covariate design?”. Best descriptive / ANOVA-style APA reporting. two methods coincide trivially covariates NULL, also covariates numeric logical (factor levels expand ). diverge categorical covariate proportions skewed observed data.
code: # Same model, balanced (emmeans-style) marginal means: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   adjustment = "balanced",   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   69.93      73.59          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.04      25.62          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.08       5.23     <.001  0.22  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (balanced). #> Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Adding covariates changes effect sizes remain meaningful: effect_size = "f2" "omega2" reported partial f² / partial ω² via stats::drop1() restricted focal term — correct generalisation bivariate effect size covariate-adjusted model. effect_size = "d" "g" raise spicy_unsupported: Cohen’s d Hedges’ g defined extension adjustment (pooled SD canonical analogue). error message points f2 / omega2 partial-F generalisations. effect-size variants noncentral CIs (effect_size_ci = TRUE) computed fitted model displayed coefficients remain invariant vcov (HC* / CR* / bootstrap / jackknife affect contrast SE).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: covariates accepts additive terms . Formula syntax interactions transforms (covariates = ~ age * sex, covariates = ~ (age^2)) raises spicy_unsupported migration hint. Interactions polynomials reserved future spicy release. fit fully-specified model right now, lm() directly inspect via summary() table_regression() (accepts interaction / polynomial / () terms natively).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: table_continuous_lm() supports four effect sizes via effect_size argument. derived fitted model displayed coefficients R², table stays internally consistent — invariant vcov (choice classical HC* standard errors changes contrast SE, CI, test statistic standardized effect size ). Cohen’s d Hedges’ g conventions two-group comparisons (Cohen 1988; Hedges Olkin 1985; APA 2020) require exactly two non-empty levels: Hedges’ g applies small-sample correction J = 1 - 3/(4·df_resid - 1). generally preferred raw d published reports (Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018): categorical predictors three levels (numeric predictors), use Hays’ omega² less biased estimate population variance explained (Hays 1963; Olejnik Algina 2003; Lakens 2013): Cohen’s f² (= R² / (1 - R²)) effect size familiar power-analysis frameworks (e.g. G*Power) defined predictor type:
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "d" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²     d     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11  1175  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01  1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²     g     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11  1175  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01  1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   effect_size = "omega2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.68                81.56         #>  Body mass index               │        26.17                23.55         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    ω²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.10      <.001  0.21  0.21  1200  #>  Body mass index               │    26.35      <.001  0.13  0.13  1188 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   effect_size = "f2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                      │  B    95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    f²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.04    -0.02      0.10      .177  0.00  0.00  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.04     0.02      0.05     <.001  0.02  0.02  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Setting effect_size_ci = TRUE adds confidence interval effect size. implementation uses modern noncentral-distribution inversion approach — consensus standard commercial statistical software (Stata esize / estat esize, SAS PROC TTEST PROC GLM EFFECTSIZE) mainstream R packages (effectsize, MOTE, TOSTER, effsize): Noncentral t inversion "d" "g" (Steiger Fouladi 1997; Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018; Cousineau Goulet-Pelletier 2021), nominal coverage across sample sizes — unlike older Hedges-Olkin normal approximation biased small samples. Noncentral F inversion "omega2" "f2" (Steiger 2004; Smithson 2003). printed rendered outputs, effect size displayed bracketed CI (article-ready): CI level follows ci_level (default 0.95). programmatic access, wide raw output exposes separate numeric columns, long output always includes : weights supplied, four effect sizes (CIs) computed weighted least-squares fit, keeping consistent weighted contrast CI (DuMouchel Duncan 1983). right convention case-weighted reporting; propensity-score balance assessment (Austin Stuart 2015) complex-survey designs, dedicated packages (cobalt::bal.tab() survey) appropriate.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²            g           #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11 [-0.25, 0.03]  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01 [-0.15, 0.13]  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1175  #>  Body mass index               │ 1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>                                      Variable   M (No)  M (Yes) Δ (Yes - No) #> wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) 69.36317 67.64538    -1.717793 #>                 95% CI LL 95% CI UL         p          R²          g #> wellbeing_score -3.906437 0.4708504 0.1238546 0.002017481 -0.1098571 #>                 effect_size_ci_lower effect_size_ci_upper    n #> wellbeing_score           -0.2497291           0.03006164 1175 out <- table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   output = "long" ) out[, c("variable", "es_type", "es_value", "es_ci_lower", "es_ci_upper")] #>          variable es_type   es_value es_ci_lower es_ci_upper #> 1 wellbeing_score       g -0.1098571  -0.2497291  0.03006164 #> 2 wellbeing_score    <NA>         NA          NA          NA

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: Pretty outcome labels comma decimal separator (useful European reporting):
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   labels = c(     wellbeing_score = "WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100)",     bmi = "Body-mass index (kg/m²)"   ),   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   decimal_mark = "," ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL  #> ─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100) │   67,16      71,05          3,89           2,13     #>  Body-mass index (kg/m²) │   25,69      26,20          0,51           0,09     #>  #>  Variable                │ 95% CI UL    p     R²           g           n    #> ─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100) │   5,64     <,001  0,02  0,25 [0,14; 0,36]  1200  #>  Body-mass index (kg/m²) │   0,93      ,018  0,00  0,14 [0,02; 0,25]  1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text:
function supports output formats summary-table helpers, including tinytable, gt, flextable, excel, word, clipboard. #mwlefoodfi table { font-family: system-ui, ‘Segoe UI’, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, ‘Apple Color Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Symbol’, ‘Noto Color Emoji’; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale; }

#mwlefoodfi thead, #mwlefoodfi tbody, #mwlefoodfi tfoot, #mwlefoodfi tr, #mwlefoodfi td, #mwlefoodfi th { border-style: none; }

#mwlefoodfi p { margin: 0; padding: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_table { display: table; border-collapse: collapse; line-height: normal; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; color: #333333; font-size: 16px; font-weight: normal; font-style: normal; background-color: #FFFFFF; width: auto; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #A8A8A8; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #A8A8A8; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_caption { padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_title { color: #333333; font-size: 125%; font-weight: initial; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-width: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_subtitle { color: #333333; font-size: 85%; font-weight: initial; padding-top: 3px; padding-bottom: 5px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-top-color: #FFFFFF; border-top-width: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_heading { background-color: #FFFFFF; text-align: center; border-bottom-color: #FFFFFF; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_bottom_border { border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_col_headings { border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_col_heading { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; text-transform: inherit; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: bottom; padding-top: 5px; padding-bottom: 6px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; overflow-x: hidden; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner_outer { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 0; padding-bottom: 0; padding-left: 4px; padding-right: 4px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner_outer:first-child { padding-left: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner_outer:last-child { padding-right: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner { border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: bottom; padding-top: 5px; padding-bottom: 5px; overflow-x: hidden; display: inline-block; width: 100%; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_spanner_row { border-bottom-style: hidden; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_group_heading { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 2px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; text-align: left; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_empty_group_heading { padding: 0.5px; color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 2px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_from_md > :first-child { margin-top: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_from_md > :last-child { margin-bottom: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; margin: 10px; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 1px; border-top-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; overflow-x: hidden; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_stub { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-right-style: solid; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_stub_row_group { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-right-style: solid; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; vertical-align: top; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_row_group_first td { border-top-width: 2px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_row_group_first th { border-top-width: 2px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_summary_row { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_first_summary_row { border-top-style: solid; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_first_summary_row.thick { border-top-width: 2px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_last_summary_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_grand_summary_row { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_first_grand_summary_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-top-style: double; border-top-width: 6px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_last_grand_summary_row_top { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-style: double; border-bottom-width: 6px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_striped { background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_table_body { border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_footnotes { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-style: none; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_footnote { margin: 0px; font-size: 90%; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_sourcenotes { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-style: none; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_sourcenote { font-size: 90%; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_left { text-align: left; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_center { text-align: center; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_right { text-align: right; font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_font_normal { font-weight: normal; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_font_bold { font-weight: bold; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_font_italic { font-style: italic; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_super { font-size: 65%; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_footnote_marks { font-size: 75%; vertical-align: 0.4em; position: initial; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_asterisk { font-size: 100%; vertical-align: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_1 { text-indent: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_2 { text-indent: 10px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_3 { text-indent: 15px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_4 { text-indent: 20px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_5 { text-indent: 25px; }

#mwlefoodfi .katex-display { display: inline-flex !important; margin-bottom: 0.75em !important; }

#mwlefoodfi div.Reactable > div.rt-table > div.rt-thead > div.rt-tr.rt-tr-group-header > div.rt-th-group:{ height: 0px !important; }

.gt_table thead tr:first-child { border-top: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table thead tr.gt_spanner_row { border-bottom-style: none !important; }

.gt_table thead th, .gt_table thead td { background-color: transparent !important; }

.gt_table thead tr:last-child th[id=“LL”], .gt_table thead tr:last-child th[id=“UL”] { border-top: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table thead tr:last-child { border-bottom: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table tbody tr:last-child { border-bottom: 1px solid currentColor !important; }

.gt_table tbody tr { border-top-style: none !important; border-bottom-style: none !important; }

.gt_table .gt_col_heading, .gt_table .gt_spanner { white-space: nowrap !important; }

.gt_table .gt_row .gt_right, .gt_table .gt_row .gt_center { white-space: nowrap !important; }

.gt_table, .gt_heading, .gt_col_headings, .gt_col_heading, .gt_column_spanner_outer, .gt_column_spanner, .gt_title, .gt_subtitle, .gt_sourcenotes, .gt_sourcenote { background-color: transparent !important; color: currentColor !important; }
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth,     select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi, life_sat_health),     by = sex,     vcov = "HC3",     statistic = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: table_continuous_lm() returns object can coerced plain data.frame / tbl_df (stripping spicy formatting attributes) piped broom::tidy() / broom::glance() downstream tidyverse-stats workflow:
code: out <- table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  #>  #>  Variable                      │         g           n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.25 [0.14, 0.36]  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.14 [0.02, 0.25]  1188  # One row per estimated parameter: emmean per level, contrast for # binary predictors, slope for numeric predictors. broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   outcome        label term  estimate_type estimate std.error conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>          <chr> <chr> <chr>            <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 wellbeing_sco… WHO-… Fema… emmean          67.2       0.623  65.9       68.4   #> 2 wellbeing_sco… WHO-… Male  emmean          71.0       0.644  69.8       72.3   #> 3 wellbeing_sco… WHO-… Male… difference       3.89      0.896   2.13       5.64  #> 4 bmi            Body… Fema… emmean          25.7       0.150  25.4       26.0   #> 5 bmi            Body… Male  emmean          26.2       0.155  25.9       26.5   #> 6 bmi            Body… Male… difference       0.512     0.216   0.0888     0.935 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: statistic <dbl>, p.value <dbl>  # One row per outcome with model-level statistics: r.squared, # adj.r.squared, F / t, df, p.value, nobs, weighted_n, plus the # effect-size summary. broom::glance(out) #> # A tibble: 2 × 16 #>   outcome     label predictor_type test_type statistic    df df.residual p.value #>   <chr>       <chr> <chr>          <chr>         <dbl> <int>       <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 wellbeing_… WHO-… categorical    F             18.8      1        1198 1.55e-5 #> 2 bmi         Body… categorical    F              5.64     1        1186 1.78e-2 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: r.squared <dbl>, adj.r.squared <dbl>, es_type <chr>, #> #   es_value <dbl>, es_ci_lower <dbl>, es_ci_upper <dbl>, nobs <int>, #> #   weighted_n <dbl>

title: Model-based continuous summary tables
text: See vignette("table-continuous", package = "spicy") descriptive continuous summary tables classical group-comparison tests. See vignette("table-regression", package = "spicy") full coefficient table analysis moves per-outcome one-predictor model single multi-predictor lm / glm, including interactions, polynomials, standardised coefficients. See vignette("summary-tables-reporting", package = "spicy") cross-function reporting workflow using summary-table helpers along APA Table 1 / 2 / 3 sequence.

title: Continuous summary tables
text: Use select choose continuous variables want summarize: omit select, table_continuous() scans data frame keeps numeric columns:

title: Continuous summary tables
text: Add summarize variables across categories. supplied, Welch-test p-value column added automatically: main pattern reporting continuous variables across groups education, sex, treatment arm, survey wave. Pass p_value = FALSE suppress test column keep output strictly descriptive. want outcomes reported linear-model workflow, heteroskedasticity-consistent cluster-robust standard errors, case weights, additive covariate adjustment, use table_continuous_lm() instead:

what: Add test statistics and effect sizes
title: Continuous summary tables
text: Grouped tables can also report test statistic effect size alongside default p-value column: Use test = "student" equal-variance parametric tests test = "nonparametric" rank-based comparisons: effect_size = TRUE auto-selects canonical measure chosen test number groups: Hedges’ g (parametric, 2 groups), eta-squared (parametric, 3+ groups), rank-biserial r (nonparametric, 2 groups), epsilon-squared (nonparametric, 3+ groups). pick specific measure explicitly, pass character name: Allowed values "none" (default), "auto" (= legacy TRUE), "hedges_g", "eta_sq", "r_rb", "epsilon_sq". Incompatible explicit choices (e.g. "eta_sq" two groups, "hedges_g" test = "nonparametric") trigger actionable error. need underlying columns processing, use output = "data.frame":

title: Continuous summary tables
text: select supports tidyselect helpers: programmatic selection, set regex = TRUE: Use exclude want broad selection one two explicit removals:

title: Continuous summary tables
text: Use labels replace technical variable names reporting labels: table_continuous() supports reporting-oriented outputs table_categorical(): example, tinytable works well Quarto R Markdown documents:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     labels = c(       bmi = "Body mass index",       wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",       life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"     ),     output = "gt"   ) ) table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   output = "tinytable" )

title: Continuous summary tables
text: Use function export table directly:
code: table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   output = "excel",   excel_path = "table_continuous.xlsx" )  table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   output = "word",   word_path = "table_continuous.docx" )

title: Continuous summary tables
text: printed ASCII table every rendered output share formatting vocabulary table_continuous_lm() table_categorical(): align = "decimal" (default) aligns numeric columns decimal mark, matching SPSS / SAS / LaTeX siunitx conventions. "center", "right", legacy "auto" alternatives. p_digits = 3 (default, APA standard) drives displayed precision p column small-p threshold (p_digits = 4 -> .0451 <.0001). show_n = FALSE ci = FALSE drop corresponding columns (CI spanner / borders) structurally every output; underlying n ci_lower / ci_upper always present output = "data.frame" / "long" downstream code.
code: table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = sex,   ci = FALSE,   show_n = FALSE,   p_digits = 4 ) #> Descriptive statistics #>  #>  Variable                      │ Group     M     SD     Min    Max      p     #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ Female  67.16  14.80  19.60  100.00  <.0001  #>                                │ Male    71.05  16.23  18.70  100.00

title: Continuous summary tables

See vignette("table-continuous-lm", package = "spicy") model-based continuous summary tables robust standard errors case weights. See vignette("table-regression", package = "spicy") full coefficient tables one several fitted lm / glm models (APA Table 3). See vignette("summary-tables-reporting", package = "spicy") cross-function reporting workflow ties four summary-table helpers together along APA Table 1 / 2 / 3 sequence.

title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: Poisson regression models rate events log link, exponentiate = TRUE turns coefficient incidence rate ratio (IRR) — multiplicative effect expected count. Real count data usually violate Poisson assumption one two ways: conditional variance exceeds conditional mean (overdispersion — negative binomial’s job), zeros count process predicts (excess zeros — job zero-inflated hurdle models, add second, binary submodel zeros). two entangled: unmodelled dispersion surfaces apparent excess zeros, model comparison end vignette, raw zero count, arbitrates. extension changes table must show, sections follow escalation.

title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: IRR multiplies expected article count, holding predictors constant: expected count women 20% lower otherwise-similar men (IRR 0.80), additional mentor publication raises student’s expected count 2.6% (IRR 1.03 displayed; exp(0.0256) = 1.026). Inference Wald-z link scale; footer records SE CI conventions. One remark rate data: observation window fixed three years everyone, exposure adjustment needed. unequal exposure — person-years, plot area, time risk — add offset(log(exposure)) formula usual; offset absorbed silently (spurious coefficient row). model targets rate per unit exposure — articles per person-year, counts per hectare — exponentiated coefficients still rate ratios, read exactly .
code: fit_pois <- glm(art ~ fem + mar + kid5 + ment, data = bioChemists,                 family = poisson()) table_regression(fit_pois, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Poisson regression: art #>  #>  Variable        │   IRR     SE      95% CI       p    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │    1.41  0.08  [1.26, 1.59]  <.001  #>  fem:            │                                     #>    Men (ref.)    │     –     –         –         –     #>    Women         │    0.80  0.04  [0.72, 0.89]  <.001  #>  mar:            │                                     #>    Single (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Married       │    1.16  0.07  [1.03, 1.31]   .013  #>  kid5            │    0.83  0.03  [0.77, 0.90]  <.001  #>  ment            │    1.03  0.00  [1.02, 1.03]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │  915                                #>  R² (McFadden)   │    0.05                             #>  R² (Nagelkerke) │    0.19                             #>  AIC             │ 3312.3                              #>  #> Note. Poisson regression. #> Std. errors: classical (Fisher information). #> IRR = incidence rate ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as IRR; SE on the IRR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: Poisson regression assumes conditional variance equals conditional mean. raw comparison marginal moments establish otherwise — covariates alone push marginal variance marginal mean — check belongs fitted model: Pearson dispersion statistic Poisson fit 1.82, equidispersion give roughly 1. Poisson model answers overdispersion understating standard errors. MASS::glm.nb() fits NB2 model, adding dispersion parameter \(\theta\) \(V(Y \mid x) = \mu + \mu^2/\theta\) — smaller \(\theta\) means stronger overdispersion, Poisson limiting case \(\theta\) grows without bound: summary(fit_nb) reports \(\hat\theta\) = 2.26 (SE 0.27); readers trained Stata know reciprocal, \(\alpha = 1/\theta\) = 0.44 — available opt-fit-stat rows via show_fit_stats = c("nobs", "theta", "alpha", "AIC"), table can carry dispersion estimate . likelihood-ratio test Poisson restriction — boundary test, p-value halved — rejects decisively (LR = 180.3, p < .001). glmmTMB::nbinom2, used mixed-model section , NB2 parameterization. IRRs barely move, inference : evidence marriage weakens (p = .013 Poisson, p = .072 ) — Poisson significance artifact ignored overdispersion. AIC falls 3312.3 3134.1, comparison returns .
code: c(mean = mean(bioChemists$art), var = var(bioChemists$art)) #>     mean      var  #> 1.692896 3.709742 sum(residuals(fit_pois, "pearson")^2) / df.residual(fit_pois) #> [1] 1.824161 fit_nb <- MASS::glm.nb(art ~ fem + mar + kid5 + ment, data = bioChemists) table_regression(fit_nb, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Negative-binomial regression: art #>  #>  Variable        │   IRR     SE      95% CI       p    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │    1.35  0.11  [1.15, 1.59]  <.001  #>  fem:            │                                     #>    Men (ref.)    │     –     –         –         –     #>    Women         │    0.81  0.06  [0.70, 0.93]   .003  #>  mar:            │                                     #>    Single (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Married       │    1.16  0.09  [0.99, 1.36]   .072  #>  kid5            │    0.84  0.04  [0.76, 0.93]  <.001  #>  ment            │    1.03  0.00  [1.02, 1.04]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │  915                                #>  R² (McFadden)   │    0.03                             #>  R² (Nagelkerke) │    0.11                             #>  AIC             │ 3134.1                              #>  #> Note. Negative-binomial regression. #> Std. errors: Model-based (asymptotic). #> IRR = incidence rate ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as IRR; SE on the IRR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: Two-part models tell two different stories zeros, difference matters interpretation: zero-inflated model (zeroinfl(); Lambert 1992) says zeros structural: come latent group never publish, regardless count process. zero component models probability structural zero. hurdle model (hurdle(); Mullahy 1986) says zeros single source, hurdle: first publish ; truncated count process decides many. zero component models probability nonzero count — opposite direction. table_regression() renders count coefficients first zero component labelled block, footer line stating exactly block models. exponentiate = TRUE exponentiation per block: count coefficients become IRRs underlying count process — zeroinfl, rate among units structural-zero class; hurdle, latent untruncated rate — multiplicative effects overall expected count E(Y); zero component’s logit coefficients become odds ratios. (single number E(Y) scale, see combined AME .) two-part formula segment | specifies zero model. four covariates drive much publishers publish, mentor’s productivity governs membership never-publishing class — deliberate restriction full inflation equation (Zeileis et al. 2008). inflation equation substantive hypothesis specify, default accept. Compare ment row two zero blocks — predictor, substantive finding, opposite-looking odds ratios: Zero-inflation block: 0.88 — mentor publication lowers odds structural zero. Zero hurdle block: 1.08 — mentor publication raises odds publishing . blocks say thing: productive mentor makes observed zero less likely. Reading either number without block label invites sign error; block labels footer lines exist prevent . choosing two: AIC leans toward zero-inflated variant (3225.5 vs 3233.8; two models number parameters, pure likelihood comparison) — though Poisson two-part models still trail plain negative binomial previous section, comparison next section takes . real choice substantive, statistical: information criterion can tell whether zeros structural sampling zeros. Zero-inflation fits units plausibly can never experience event (latent never-publisher class); hurdle fits one immune — zeros arise single --nothing first stage, one process, covariates, deciding whether anything published . One mechanical difference also separates : zero inflation can add zeros count process, whereas hurdle frees zero probability either direction — alone can accommodate fewer zeros count process implies (Cameron & Trivedi 2013). Zero-component coefficients test substantive hypotheses: take significance stars, p_adjust corrections treat part family count-component coefficients. display count part, set show_components = FALSE (model still estimated full).
code: fit_zip <- zeroinfl(art ~ fem + mar + kid5 + ment | ment,                     data = bioChemists) table_regression(fit_zip, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Poisson zero-inflated regression: art #>  #>  Variable        │   IRR     SE      95% CI       p    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │    1.84  0.12  [1.61, 2.10]  <.001  #>  fem:            │                                     #>    Men (ref.)    │     –     –         –         –     #>    Women         │    0.80  0.05  [0.72, 0.90]  <.001  #>  mar:            │                                     #>    Single (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Married       │    1.14  0.08  [1.01, 1.30]   .041  #>  kid5            │    0.85  0.04  [0.78, 0.93]  <.001  #>  ment            │    1.02  0.00  [1.01, 1.02]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Zero-inflation: │                                     #>    (Intercept)   │    0.50  0.10  [0.34, 0.75]  <.001  #>    ment          │    0.88  0.04  [0.81, 0.95]   .001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │  915                                #>  AIC             │ 3225.5                              #>  #> Note. Poisson zero-inflated regression. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Zero-inflation component: log-odds of a structural (excess) zero. Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as odds ratios. #> IRR = incidence rate ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as IRR; SE on the IRR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric). fit_hur <- hurdle(art ~ fem + mar + kid5 + ment | ment,                   data = bioChemists) table_regression(fit_hur, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Poisson hurdle regression: art #>  #>  Variable        │   IRR     SE      95% CI       p    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │    1.88  0.13  [1.63, 2.16]  <.001  #>  fem:            │                                     #>    Men (ref.)    │     –     –         –         –     #>    Women         │    0.80  0.05  [0.70, 0.90]  <.001  #>  mar:            │                                     #>    Single (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Married       │    1.10  0.08  [0.96, 1.27]   .169  #>  kid5            │    0.87  0.04  [0.79, 0.95]   .003  #>  ment            │    1.02  0.00  [1.01, 1.02]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Zero hurdle:    │                                     #>    (Intercept)   │    1.28  0.14  [1.04, 1.58]   .021  #>    ment          │    1.08  0.01  [1.06, 1.11]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │  915                                #>  AIC             │ 3233.8                              #>  #> Note. Poisson hurdle regression. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Zero hurdle component: log-odds of a nonzero count. what: Which count model? Side by side
title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: comparing likelihoods, run diagnostic zero question actually asks: zeros fitted count model implies? Compare observed zero share share model predicts: Poisson underpredicts zeros badly (0.21 0.30 observed), negative binomial reproduces almost exactly (0.30) — without zero component . apparent excess zeros overdispersion. point Long (1997) Cameron & Trivedi (2013) make, AIC row now confirms . (-standard Vuong test used : longer recommended testing zero-inflation — Wilson 2015 — information criteria substantive reasoning carry comparison.) Read AIC row across. ZIP’s entire inflation component buys less negative binomial’s single dispersion parameter (3225.5 3134.1): modelling zeros directly worse answer overdispersion . top negative binomial, inflation part adds modest gain (3134.1 → 3122.5). hurdle (AIC 3233.8) sits ZIP, previous section argued choosing two substantive, fit-based. zero block simply stays empty columns models none, substantively IRRs agree across four — changes inference account zeros.
code: c(observed = mean(bioChemists$art == 0),   poisson  = mean(dpois(0, fitted(fit_pois))),   negbin   = mean(dnbinom(0, mu = fitted(fit_nb), size = fit_nb$theta))) #>  observed   poisson    negbin  #> 0.3005464 0.2090480 0.3035314 fit_zinb <- zeroinfl(art ~ fem + mar + kid5 + ment | ment,                      data = bioChemists, dist = "negbin") table_regression(   list(Poisson = fit_pois, ZIP = fit_zip, NB = fit_nb, ZINB = fit_zinb),   show_columns = "b", exponentiate = TRUE ) #> Regression comparison: art #>  #>                    Poisson    ZIP      NB      ZINB    #>                    ───────  ───────  ───────  ───────  #>  Variable        │   IRR      IRR      IRR      IRR    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │    1.41     1.84     1.35     1.51  #>  fem:            │                                     #>    Men (ref.)    │     –        –        –        –    #>    Women         │    0.80     0.80     0.81     0.81  #>  mar:            │                                     #>    Single (ref.) │     –        –        –        –    #>    Married       │    1.16     1.14     1.16     1.15  #>  kid5            │    0.83     0.85     0.84     0.85  #>  ment            │    1.03     1.02     1.03     1.02  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Zero-inflation: │                                     #>    (Intercept)   │             0.50              0.45  #>    ment          │             0.88              0.54  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │  915      915      915      915     #>  R² (McFadden)   │    0.05              0.03           #>  R² (Nagelkerke) │    0.19              0.11           #>  AIC             │ 3312.3   3225.5   3134.1   3122.5   #>  #> Note. Model 1: Poisson regression; Model 2: Poisson zero-inflated regression; Model 3: negative-binomial regression; Model 4: negative-binomial zero-inflated regression. #> Std. errors: #>   Model 1: classical (Fisher information) #>   Model 2: Wald asymptotic (z) #>   Model 3: Model-based (asymptotic) #>   Model 4: Wald asymptotic (z) #> Zero-inflation component: log-odds of a structural (excess) zero. title: Count and two-part regression tables

title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: Students cluster labs, cohorts, mentor groups. CR* family covers components one estimator — sandwich::vcovCL() full score matrix, count zero rows shift together. two-part models every CR* variant maps estimator, footer records CL: read shift effect clustering. labs assigned random, carry real dependence: movement marriage (p = .041 → .147) comes sandwich estimator longer trusting ZIP’s model-based variance — unclustered robust SEs give p ≈ .11 — rest noise synthetic labels. example demonstrates mechanical: components shift together one CL estimator. genuinely clustered data, positive within-cluster dependence widens intervals , borderline effects typically first casualties. HC* resampling estimators two-part backend refused clear error.
code: set.seed(1) bioChemists$lab <- factor(sample(1:60, nrow(bioChemists), replace = TRUE)) table_regression(fit_zip, vcov = "CR0", cluster = bioChemists$lab,                  show_columns = c("b", "se", "p")) #> Poisson zero-inflated regression: art #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE     p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │    0.61  0.09  <.001  #>  fem:            │                       #>    Men (ref.)    │     –     –     –     #>    Women         │   -0.22  0.07  <.001  #>  mar:            │                       #>    Single (ref.) │     –     –     –     #>    Married       │    0.13  0.09   .147  #>  kid5            │   -0.16  0.07   .013  #>  ment            │    0.02  0.00  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Zero-inflation: │                       #>    (Intercept)   │   -0.69  0.26   .010  #>    ment          │   -0.13  0.05   .017  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │  915                  #>  AIC             │ 3225.5                #>  #> Note. title: Count and two-part regression tables
text: counts also grouped, glmmTMB (Brooks et al. 2017) combines everything random effects: zero-inflation block, random-effects block, per-block exponentiation appear one table — salamander counts mined unmined streams (Price et al. 2016): Among streams structural-zero state, unmined ones host salamanders four times rate mined ones (IRR 4.34); also far less likely structural zeros first place (0.10). two components compound, overall abundance gap larger either alone — model-implied expected count 8.7 times higher unmined streams — one coherent ecological story told two parts. random-effects block conventions covered companion vignette Mixed-effects regression tables.
code: data("Salamanders", package = "glmmTMB") fit_mix <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   count ~ mined + (1 | site),   ziformula = ~ mined,   family = glmmTMB::nbinom2, data = Salamanders ) table_regression(fit_mix, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Negative-binomial mixed-effects regression (glmmTMB) (zero-inflated): count #>  #>  Variable             │   IRR     SE      95% CI       p    #> ──────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)          │    0.57  0.21  [0.28, 1.19]   .133  #>  mined:               │                                     #>    yes (ref.)         │     –     –         –         –     #>    no                 │    4.34  1.57  [2.14, 8.81]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Zero-inflation:      │                                     #>    (Intercept)        │    1.27  0.62  [0.49, 3.30]   .621  #>    mined: yes (ref.)  │     –     –         –         –     #>    mined: no          │    0.10  0.08  [0.02, 0.49]   .004  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Random effects:      │                                     #>    σ site (Intercept) │    0.37  0.17  [0.16, 0.81]   –     #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                    │  644                                #>  N (site)             │   23                                #>  R² (marginal)        │    0.44                             #>  R² (conditional)     │    0.56                             #>  AIC                  │ 1741.4                              #>  BIC                  │ 1768.2                              #>  #> Note. Negative-binomial mixed-effects regression (glmmTMB) (zero-inflated). #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> p-values: Wald-z asymptotic (glmmTMB). #> Random effects (ML): LR test vs nbinom2 regression, χ̄²(1) = 5.37, p = 0.010. #> Zero-inflation component: log-odds of a structural (excess) zero. Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as odds ratios. #> IRR = incidence rate ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as IRR; SE on the IRR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

what: The mixed model in one paragraph
title: Mixed-effects regression tables
text: mixed model splits coefficients two. Fixed effects population-level slopes report regression. Random effects let selected coefficients vary across groups: (Days | Subject) gives every subject intercept Days slope, drawn common distribution. model estimates random part 18 pairs coefficients variance components distribution — much subjects differ baseline, much slopes differ, two go together. regression table therefore needs two kinds rows, exactly table_regression() prints.

title: Mixed-effects regression tables
text: Reading table, top bottom: Fixed effects first, regression table. lmerTest loaded, inference t-test Satterthwaite degrees freedom — lmerTest default (Kuznetsova et al. 2017), offered SAS PROC MIXED DDFM=SATTERTHWAITE, method small-sample comparison Luke (2017) recommends groups — footer says . Random effects: block reports variance components rows, SE CI: -subject SD intercept Days slope (σ), correlation (ρ), residual SD. SD scale default SD scale coefficient modifies (Gelman & Hill 2007) — ms intercept, ms per day Days slope: sleep deprivation adds 10.5 ms per day average (fixed Days), individual slopes spread around mean SD 5.9 ms per day. p column random-effect rows shows dash, design — see next section. rule: n (observations), N (Subject) (groups), Nakagawa marginal / conditional R² — variance explained fixed effects alone versus fixed random together (Nakagawa & Schielzeth 2013). gap two (0.28 vs 0.80 ) summary much grouping structure matters. footer closes likelihood-ratio test whole random part model without random effects, referred chi-bar-squared mixture distribution.
