title: Ordinal regression tables
text:
default console table shown one several targets. output argument also produces raw data frame, long broom-style tibble, — corresponding Suggests package — rich gt, flextable, tinytable, Excel, Word tables. structure (per-category AME matrix included) carries every format. #mwlefoodfi table { font-family: system-ui, ‘Segoe UI’, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, ‘Apple Color Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Symbol’, ‘Noto Color Emoji’; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale; }

title: Ordinal regression tables
text: Agresti, . (2010). Analysis Ordinal Categorical Data (2nd ed.). Wiley. Brant, R. (1990). Assessing proportionality proportional odds model ordinal logistic regression. Biometrics, 46(4), 1171–1178. Long, J. S., & Freese, J. (2014). Regression Models Categorical Dependent Variables Using Stata (3rd ed.). Stata Press. Peterson, B., & Harrell, F. E. (1990). Partial proportional odds models ordinal response variables. Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series C (Applied Statistics), 39(2), 205–217. Williams, R. (2012). Interpret Statistical Models Using marginaleffects R Python. Journal Statistical Software.

what: The Cox model in one paragraph
title: Survival regression tables
text: Cox model works hazard — instantaneous event rate among still risk. one-unit increase predictor multiplies hazard constant factor — exponentiated coefficient — every point time (proportional-hazards assumption), baseline hazard left completely unspecified: semiparametric trick made model ubiquitous applied work (Cox 1972; Therneau & Grambsch 2000). Everything also assumes censoring uninformative: given covariates, subjects censored time t must representative still risk t — assumption observed data verify. Two consequences shape table. intercept row — baseline hazard absorbs — residual variance, model-fit block reports survival reader expects instead: number events concordance.

title: Survival regression tables
text: Reading table: exponentiate = TRUE turns log-hazard coefficient hazard ratio. Women’s hazard 0.57 times men’s — 43% lower hazard given moment, else equal; ECOG point multiplies hazard 1.60. Leave exponentiate log-hazard B column. Inference Wald-z log scale, matching summary.coxph() Stata stcox; statistic p-value invariant exponentiation. footer reports events (163 deaths among 226 patients — precision power survival model governed chiefly event count, n) concordance C = 0.64: probability , two comparable patients, one predicted fail earlier actually . 0.5 coin flip; 0.64 modest discrimination (Harrell 2015). table reports model, assumption: check proportionality survival::cox.zph() publishing (Therneau & Grambsch 2000): covariate gets test proportional-hazards assumption, plus global test. smallest p .12 (sex) global test gives p = .17 — little evidence proportionality. test rejects, two standard remedies flow table without ceremony: stratify offending categorical factor (strata() terms absorbed baseline hazard, coefficient row) give covariate time-varying effect via counting-process data (Surv(tstart, tstop, event)); render like fit.
code: cx <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung2) table_regression(cx, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Cox proportional hazards regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>  Variable      │   HR      SE      95% CI       p    #> ───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  age           │    1.01  0.01  [0.99, 1.03]   .225  #>  sex:          │                                     #>    Male (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Female      │    0.57  0.10  [0.41, 0.80]  <.001  #>  ph.ecog       │    1.60  0.18  [1.28, 2.00]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226                                #>  N events      │  163                                #>  AIC           │ 1454.2                              #>  #> Note. Cox proportional hazards regression. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Concordance C = 0.64 (SE = 0.03). #> HR = hazard ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as HR; SE on the HR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric). cox.zph(cx) #>         chisq df    p #> age     0.244  1 0.62 #> sex     2.475  1 0.12 #> ph.ecog 2.353  1 0.13 #> GLOBAL  4.975  3 0.17

what: Why there is no AME column
title: Survival regression tables
text: "ame" request Cox fit refused explanation rather silently honoured: average marginal effect needs response scale, Cox model deliberately commit one — per-coefficient “effect survival” ambiguous (time? scale?), delta-method standard errors generic AME machinery attach unreliable Cox fits. hazard ratio per-coefficient summary model. Absolute-scale summaries exist, require analyst choose time horizon first — exactly next section , bootstrap inference place delta method.

what: Absolute effects: RMST and risk differences
title: Survival regression tables
text: hazard ratio answers “relative rate?”, never “much longer?” “many fewer?”. Women’s hazard 0.57 times men’s — many extra months life , many fewer deaths end first year? questions patients policy makers actually ask, methodological literature increasingly calls answers reported alongside hazard ratio (Royston & Parmar 2013; Uno et al. 2014). also settle workflow question assumption check left open: cox.zph() rejects proportionality, hazard ratio degrades follow--weighted average changing effect, RMST difference [0, tau] remains well-defined estimand — case absolute summaries lead report rather accompany . Two absolute summaries job: restricted mean survival time (RMST) difference: difference mean event-free time fixed window [0, tau] — area two survival curves tau; risk difference: difference probability event landmark time. table_regression() computes fitted Cox model g-computation (regression standardization): every patient’s survival curve predicted twice, level predictor, keeping covariates observed; curves averaged, two standardized curves integrated tau (RMST) evaluated at_time complemented (risk = 1 − survival). Standard errors come nonparametric bootstrap, expect chunk take seconds; 200 replicates keep vignette light, default boot_n = 1000 better choice publication table. absolute columns re-express model interpretable scales, averaged sample’s covariate mix. cuts ways: computed fit, inherit assumptions — proportionality doubt, estimate RMST nonparametrically (per-arm Kaplan–Meier, survRM2) rather standardizing misspecified model. female adds average 40.5 event-free days first year (95% CI 17 64) lowers one-year risk death 19 percentage points (95% CI 8 30); additional ECOG point costs 38 days adds 15 percentage points one-year risk. age row teaches contrast unit matters much scale: per year age effect invisible (HR 1.01, day RMST), per decade — clinically natural unit — fit implies HR 1.12 nine fewer event-free days. Rescale predictor per-unit contrast meaningful one. Two design decisions shape columns. First, horizon mandatory: RMST without tau estimand, table_regression() refuses guess — pass clinically meaningful time, tau = "minmax" largest window every compared group can support (value used disclosed table note). Second, contrast level reference factor predictors +1-unit shift continuous predictors, mirroring AME convention model families — columns read way across tables.
code: set.seed(7) table_regression(cx,                  show_columns = c("b", "rmst", "rmst_ci",                                   "risk_diff", "risk_diff_ci"),                  tau = 365, at_time = 365,                  exponentiate = TRUE, boot_n = 200) #> Cox proportional hazards regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>  Variable      │   HR     dRMST (365)       95% CI       dRisk (365)  #> ───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  age           │    1.01        -0.84  [ -2.27,   0.60]         0.00  #>  sex:          │                                                      #>    Male (ref.) │     –                                                #>    Female      │    0.57        40.50  [ 16.65,  64.35]        -0.19  #>  ph.ecog       │    1.60       -38.21  [-58.73, -17.70]         0.15  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226                                                 #>  N events      │  163                                                 #>  AIC           │ 1454.2                                               #>  #>  Variable      │     95% CI      #> ───────────────┼──────────────── #>  age           │ [-0.00,  0.01]  #>  sex:          │                 #>    Male (ref.) │                 #>    Female      │ [-0.30, -0.08]  #>  ph.ecog       │ [ 0.08,  0.22]  #>  #> Note. Cox proportional hazards regression. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Concordance C = 0.64 (SE = 0.03). #> dRMST = difference in restricted mean survival time over [0, 365]; dRisk = difference in cumulative incidence at 365; adjusted by g-computation from the fitted model, SEs by nonparametric bootstrap (200 replicates). #> HR = hazard ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as HR; CI bounds exponentiated.

title: Survival regression tables
text: lung patients enrolled 18 institutions, outcomes within institution may correlate. CR* request routes Lin & Wei (1989) robust variance — grouped score (dfbeta) sandwich coxph(..., cluster = ) Stata stcox, vce(cluster) use: Two lessons . First, mechanics: cluster variable must provide one value per row model data — declaring analytic sample front () keeps cluster aligned predictors carry missing values. Second, direction: robust standard errors always larger — sex SE shrinks . Clustering corrects variance whichever direction within-centre correlation points; treating conservative inflation ritual misreads . robust variance corrects standard errors leaving estimates marginal (population-averaged); modeling alternative shared frailty term — coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog + frailty(inst), data = lung2) — whose hazard ratios instead conditional centre effect (Therneau & Grambsch 2000, ch. 9). estimand analysis needs decides .
code: table_regression(cx, vcov = "CR0", cluster = ~inst,                  exponentiate = TRUE) #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'clubSandwich': #>   method    from     #>   bread.mlm sandwich #> Cox proportional hazards regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>  Variable      │   HR      SE      95% CI       p    #> ───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  age           │    1.01  0.01  [1.00, 1.03]   .103  #>  sex:          │                                     #>    Male (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Female      │    0.57  0.06  [0.46, 0.71]  <.001  #>  ph.ecog       │    1.60  0.19  [1.27, 2.01]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226                                #>  N events      │  163                                #>  AIC           │ 1454.2                              #>  #> Note. Cox proportional hazards regression. #> Std. errors: cluster-robust (Lin-Wei), clusters by inst. #> Concordance C = 0.64 (SE = 0.03). #> HR = hazard ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as HR; SE on the HR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

title: Survival regression tables
text: adding performance score improve age--sex model? nested = TRUE compares adjacent models partial-likelihood ratio test (anova.coxph() convention) appends change rows: Adding ph.ecog buys chi-squared change 16.78 one degree freedom (p < .001), AIC drop 14.8, rise concordance 0.60 0.64 — three readings improvement, likelihood, information, discrimination scales respectively.
code: cx0 <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung2) table_regression(list(cx0, cx), nested = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE,                  show_columns = c("b", "p")) #> Hierarchical Cox proportional hazards regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>                     Model 1          Model 2      #>                  ──────────────  ───────────────  #>  Variable      │   HR       p       HR       p    #> ───────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  age           │    1.02   .062     1.01    .225  #>  sex:          │                                  #>    Male (ref.) │     –     –         –      –     #>    Female      │    0.60   .003     0.57   <.001  #>  ph.ecog       │                    1.60   <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226             226             #>  N events      │  163             163             #>  AIC           │ 1469.0          1454.2           #>  Δχ²           │     –            +16.78          #>  p (change)    │     –              <.001         #>  #> Note. Cox proportional hazards regression models. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Model 1: Concordance C = 0.60 (SE = 0.03). #> Model 2: Concordance C = 0.64 (SE = 0.03). #> HR = hazard ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as HR; CI bounds exponentiated.

title: Survival regression tables
text: Epidemiological analyses often begin one step earlier: multivariable model, candidate predictor examined Cox model, two stages reported side side. table_regression_uv() runs workflow one call — outcome takes Surv() expression, column group fitted, n events counted, estimation sample: Events/N column carries information survival reader checks first — 53 90 women died 110 136 men — paired columns support screen’s real question: adjustment move estimate? Age instructive row. Alone looks significant (HR 1.02, p = .041); adjusted sex performance status (p = .225) — univariable signal partly carried worse performance scores older patients. Reading two columns together protects final model false leads premature exclusions: predictor enter leave univariable p-value alone. Two cautions complete reading. comparison clean analytic sample declared front: every model uses 226 rows, movement columns adjustment, sample change (missing predictor values, univariable model fits larger complete cases — N column table note disclose exactly ). estimate hazard ratio, movement adjustment proof confounding: hazard ratios non-collapsible, adding strongly prognostic covariate shifts estimate even unrelated predictor.
code: table_regression_uv(lung2, outcome = Surv(time, status),                     predictors = c(age, sex, ph.ecog),                     method = "coxph", exponentiate = TRUE,                     show_columns = c("n", "n_events", "b", "ci", "p")) #> Univariable and multivariable Cox regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>                                Univariable                   Multivariable    #>                  ────────────────────────────────────────  ─────────────────  #>  Variable      │  N   Events/N   HR      95% CI       p     Events/N     HR   #> ───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  age           │ 226   163/226  1.02  [1.00, 1.04]   .041      163/226  1.01  #>  sex:          │                                                              #>    Male (ref.) │   –   110/136   –         –         –         110/136   –    #>    Female      │ 226     53/90  0.59  [0.43, 0.82]   .002        53/90  0.57  #>  ph.ecog       │ 226   163/226  1.61  [1.29, 2.02]  <.001      163/226  1.60  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │                                                 226          #>  N events      │                                                 163          #>  AIC           │                                                1454.2        #>  #>                     Multivariable     #>                  ───────────────────  #>  Variable      │    95% CI       p    #> ───────────────┼───────────────────── #>  age           │ [0.99, 1.03]   .225  #>  sex:          │                      #>    Male (ref.) │      –         –     #>    Female      │ [0.41, 0.80]  <.001  #>  ph.ecog       │ [1.28, 2.00]  <.001  #>  #> Note. Cox proportional hazards regression models. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Concordance C = 0.64 (SE = 0.03). #> HR = hazard ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as HR; CI bounds exponentiated.

title: Survival regression tables
text: parametric alternative models time (Wei 1992): covariates stretch compress survival time constant factor. better tool? audience needs time-scale reading, proportionality doubt — AFT assumption constant time ratio different assumption, weaker one — analysis needs parametric baseline, instance extrapolate beyond follow-window. exponentiate = TRUE coefficients become time ratios (TR): intercept back — parametric model must anchor time scale, exp(intercept) ≈ 801 days anchor (extrapolated characteristic time reference levels age 0), ratio despite column header. Set TR next Cox HR numbers look contradictory: women TR 1.50 HR 0.57. finding opposite scales — protective effect multiplies survival time (50% longer) multiplies hazard (43% lower). Neither direction correct; Weibull one family simultaneously AFT proportional-hazards, two scales linked exact identity within fit: HR = TR^(−1/scale) = 1.50^(−1/0.73) ≈ 0.574, scale parameter read footer — agreeing semiparametric Cox estimate (0.573 full precision, 0.57 tables display). footer names distribution interpretation depends — exponentiate produces time ratios log-scale distributions (Weibull, exponential, lognormal, loglogistic); identity-scale distribution (dist = "gaussian") request skipped warning, since coefficients already act time scale.
code: sr <- survreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung2,               dist = "weibull") table_regression(sr, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Weibull AFT regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>  Variable      │   TR       SE         95% CI          p    #> ───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)   │  800.93  343.75  [345.36, 1857.47]  <.001  #>  age           │    0.99    0.01  [  0.98,    1.01]   .261  #>  sex:          │                                            #>    Male (ref.) │     –       –            –           –     #>    Female      │    1.50    0.19  [  1.18,    1.92]   .001  #>  ph.ecog       │    0.71    0.06  [  0.60,    0.84]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226                                       #>  AIC           │ 2261.7                                     #>  #> Note. Weibull AFT regression. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Distribution: Weibull; scale = 0.73. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as TR; SE on the TR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

title: Survival regression tables
text: Analysts working rms ecosystem (Harrell 2015) — validation, calibration, nomogram tooling — fit Cox model rms::cph(), table_regression() renders layout. One requirement carried rms : cluster-robust variance uses rms::robcov(), needs model matrix response stored fit time — fit x = TRUE, y = TRUE robust request fails exactly instruction:
code: cph_fit <- rms::cph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung2,                     x = TRUE, y = TRUE) table_regression(cph_fit, vcov = "CR0", cluster = ~inst,                  exponentiate = TRUE) #> Cox proportional hazards regression (rms): Surv(time, status) #>  #>  Variable      │   HR      SE      95% CI       p    #> ───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  age           │    1.02  0.01  [1.00, 1.03]   .018  #>  sex:          │                                     #>    Male (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Female      │    0.60  0.08  [0.47, 0.78]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226                                #>  N events      │  163                                #>  AIC           │ 1469.0                              #>  #> Note. Cox proportional hazards regression (rms). #> Std. errors: cluster-robust (Lin-Wei), clusters by inst. #> HR = hazard ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as HR; SE on the HR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

title: Survival regression tables
text: flexsurv::flexsurvreg() (Jackson 2016) fits parametric survival models wide family distributions (Royston–Parmar 2002 spline models). location coefficients exponentiated generic exp(B) header — substantive reading (time ratio, hazard ratio) depends distribution, footer names together ancillary parameters: Two guardrails apply. spline model "normal" scale places coefficients probit-like scale whose exponential meaning, exponentiate = TRUE refused ; covariates ancillary parameters (anc =) act scales, exponentiate = TRUE refused whole fit rather printing meaningless ratios ancillary rows.
code: fs <- flexsurv::flexsurvreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex,                             data = lung2, dist = "weibull") table_regression(fs, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Weibull parametric survival regression: Surv(time, status) #>  #>  Variable      │ exp(B)    SE      95% CI       p    #> ───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  age           │    0.99  0.01  [0.97, 1.00]   .075  #>  sex:          │                                     #>    Male (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Female      │    1.46  0.19  [1.13, 1.87]   .003  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n             │  226                                #>  AIC           │ 2276.5                              #>  #> Note. Weibull parametric survival regression. #> Std. errors: Wald asymptotic (z). #> Distribution: Weibull; shape = 1.33, scale = 792.70. #> exp(B) = exponentiated coefficient. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as exp(B); SE on the exp(B) scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

(1992). accelerated failure time model: useful alternative Cox regression model survival analysis. Statistics Medicine, 11(14–15), 1871–1879.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: Pass fitted lm() object. default rendering returns single-model table B, SE, 95% CI, p columns fit-statistics footer (n, R², Adj.R²): Reading table: predictor occupies one row; factor predictors grouped parent variable name one row per level. reference level carries (ref.) annotation en dash across statistic columns, substantive comparison visible single glance (NEJM / BMJ clinical convention). 95% CI column reports symmetric Wald interval level set ci_level (default 0.95). CI header tracks chosen level — switching ci_level = 0.99 re-labels column accordingly. footer line names variance estimator plain English reader can find inferential regime without leaving table. estimator switches vcov (covered Robust variance ). Read substantively, one sentence per coefficient, exact numbers shown: Male — B = 3.86, 95% CI [2.08, 5.63], p < .001 — holding age smoking constant, men score average 3.9 points higher women 0–100 -5 wellbeing index. age — B = 0.05, 95% CI [-0.01, 0.11], p = .130 — interval spans effects slightly negative tenth point per year, data establish adjusted age gradient.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: Standardised coefficients (β) make predictors different natural scales comparable: one-standard-deviation increase X predicts β-standard-deviation change Y. APA Manual 7 §7.13 recommends reporting B β unstandardised effect (natural units, interpretable) stays alongside standardised effect (comparable across predictors). standardized selects method. Four available; choice consequential well-documented (Cohen, Cohen, West, Aiken 2003 §3.4; Gelman 2008): "refit" — refit model z-scored outcome predictors. Gold-standard convention, used SPSS REGRESSION Stata regress, beta. numeric dummy-coded predictors enter refit scale. "posthoc" — algebraic rescaling β = B × SD(X) / SD(Y), applied original fit. Numerically identical "refit" purely linear-additive Gaussian models; preferred refitting expensive lm() wrapped pipeline resists re-execution. "basic" — algebraic, factor dummies keep 0/1 scale rather z-scored. Useful factor levels carry meaningful base rates scale-free standardisation obscure. "smart" — Gelman’s (2008) input scaling: numeric predictors divided 2 × SD(X); binary predictors factor dummies kept 0/1 scale. resulting β change Y, SD(Y) units, two-standard-deviation change X (mean − SD mean + SD), puts continuous predictor’s ±1 SD swing footing binary’s 0 → 1 step. paper leaves Y raw, spicy divides SD(Y) β stays comparable across four methods. standardized != "none", "beta" token auto-injected show_columns immediately "b": standardised scale predictors become directly comparable: male predicts 0.25-SD higher wellbeing score, largest standardised effect model, one-SD increase age predicts change 0.04 SD. raw B column support ranking — 3.86 versus 0.05 compares factor contrast per-year slope, two different units. Caveat: standardised coefficients interactions transformed terms. model contains product term, (), poly(), log(), splines::ns() wrapper, standardised coefficient non-additive term closed-form “one-SD change X” reading (Aiken West 1991; Cohen et al. 2003 §7.7). function emits classed spicy_caveat warning runtime prints method-specific caveat line table footer, limitation exposed point use without blocking table.
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking, data = sochealth) table_regression(fit, standardized = "refit") #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B       β     SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  -0.10  1.66  [61.95, 68.45]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05   0.04  0.03  [-0.01,  0.11]   .130  #>  sex:            │                                              #>    Female (ref.) │     –      –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86   0.25  0.91  [ 2.08,  5.63]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                              #>    No (ref.)     │     –      –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  -0.11  1.11  [-3.89,  0.45]   .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                         #>  R²              │    0.02                                      #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                                      #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> β = standardised coefficient.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: predictor, can request partial effect-size column. Three measures available — Cohen’s f² (partial_f2), Pearson’s partial η² (partial_eta2), Olejnik–Algina bias-corrected partial ω² (partial_omega2). carries confidence interval derived noncentral-F inversion (Smithson 2003; Steiger 2004), exposed separate <token>_ci column. shortcuts "all_f2", "all_eta2", "all_omega2" expand point estimate together CI column. sochealth$education stored ordered factor, R codes polynomial contrasts (.L = linear, .Q = quadratic) — trend components across ordered levels, per-level effects reference. coefficient table read level--reference, convert plain factor; reuse copy throughout vignette: Methodology notes: partial F-test computed Type-II ANOVA reference (car::Anova), respects principle marginality SAS / SPSS default unbalanced designs. ω² point estimate bias-corrected via Olejnik Algina (2003) formula ((F − 1) × df1) / (F × df1 + N − df1), yielding less-biased small-sample estimator partial η². CI bounds come inverting noncentrality parameter F-distribution lower upper confidence levels (Steiger 2004 §4). Population partial η² partial ω² quantity — ω² simply less-biased small-sample estimator — columns share single Steiger interval, following MBESS convention (effectsize package instead re-inverts implied F ω², ω² bounds differ slightly). interval brackets bias-corrected point estimate, even lower bound clips zero (common near-null terms). factor predictors k levels, partial F-test joint (k − 1) df Wald test, effect-size value broadcast across non-reference dummy rows; reference row leaves effect-size cells blank.
code: sh <- sochealth |>   dplyr::mutate(education = factor(education, ordered = FALSE))  fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + education,           data = sh) table_regression(   fit,   show_columns = c("b", "p", "all_eta2", "all_omega2") ) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable                 │    B       p     η²    η² 95% CI     ω²   #> ──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │   53.96  <.001                            #>  age                      │    0.03   .344  0.00  [0.00, 0.01]  0.00  #>  sex:                     │                                           #>    Female (ref.)          │     –     –                               #>    Male                   │    3.57  <.001  0.02  [0.01, 0.03]  0.02  #>  smoking:                 │                                           #>    No (ref.)              │     –     –                               #>    Yes 0.03]  #>  smoking:                 │               #>    No (ref.)              │               #>    Yes                    │ [0.00, 0.01]  #>  education:               │               #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │               #>    Upper secondary        │ [0.17, 0.25]  #>    Tertiary               │ [0.17, 0.25]  #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> η² = partial eta-squared; ω² = bias-corrected partial omega-squared.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: B coefficient model’s structural effect — change linear predictor Xβ one-unit change x, holding predictors constant. average marginal effect (AME) observable effect response. numeric predictor average partial derivative dE[Y|X] / dx sample; factor level average discrete change — difference predicted response level reference, averaged sample (Long Freese 2014 §6.2 distinguish two). purely linear-additive lm() coincide B. models interactions transformed terms, glm() non-identity link, AME quantity substantive reader actually needs. average name load-bearing: effect computed every observation averaged sample, evaluated covariate means — marginal effect mean (MEM), point actual observation may sit. two generally differ nonlinear models; Long Freese (2014, §6.2.3) discuss choice detail. Add "ame" show_columns point estimate. Companion tokens display SE, CI, p-value: "ame_se", "ame_ci", "ame_p". shortcut "all_ame" expands full set. Reading conventions: "p" always refers B (β) coefficient, never AME. Use "ame_p" AME-specific p-value. Placing "ame" "p" keeps attribution p-value unambiguous — B-block (B / SE / p) closed AME-block opens. non-linear models AME reported response scale, link scale. Generalised linear models section works logistic-regression example. Inference delegated [marginaleffects::avg_slopes()][marginaleffects::avg_slopes] respects chosen variance estimator. cluster-robust variance (covered Robust variance), AME inference shares coefficient’s t-distribution Satterthwaite-corrected degrees freedom via clubSandwich::linear_contrast(), B AME reported inferential footing table. marginaleffects Suggests dependency; unavailable, AME columns appear header render NA without erroring (rest table unaffected).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: Pass list lm() fits. default column layout places model panel centered spanner label showing model name; sub-columns (B / SE / p) repeated spanner. dependent variables differ across models user supply labels (model_labels = named list), bare DV name lifted spanner automatically: models share DV, DV appears title. Use named list — e.g. list(Crude = m1, Adjusted = m2) — set spanner labels explicitly; pass model_labels = c(...) override names list.
code: m_wellbeing <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking,                    data = sochealth) m_bmi       <- lm(bmi             ~ age + sex + smoking,                    data = sochealth) table_regression(list(m_wellbeing, m_bmi)) #> Linear regression comparison #>  #>                      wellbeing_score             bmi           #>                    ────────────────────  ────────────────────  #>  Variable        │    B      SE     p       B      SE     p    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.66  <.001    23.98  0.40  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.03   .130     0.04  0.01  <.001  #>  sex:            │                                             #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –     –         –     –     –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.91  <.001     0.51  0.22   .018  #>  smoking:        │                                             #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –     –         –     –     –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.11   .121    -0.06  0.26   .822  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                  1163                  #>  R²              │    0.02                  0.02               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                  0.02               #>  #> Note. Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: Set nested = TRUE add -table change-statistic rows (APA Table 7.13 / Stata esttab / SPSS Model Summary convention). adjacent pair (M2 vs M1, M3 vs M2, …) contributes one column change stats R² / Adj.R²; first model column gets em-dashes (previous model compare ). Hierarchical regression requires every model sequence share analytic sample: identical nobs identical response variable. R’s listwise deletion otherwise produces different n per model soon predictor introduced M2 M3 missing values M1 see — silent bias change statistics. restrict complete cases union predictors front: Reading change rows: adding smoking Model 2 improve fit age + sex baseline (ΔR² = +0.00, F-change = 2.28, p = .132) — single added term, test equivalent test smoking coefficient (p = .132 Model 2 column). Adding bmi Model 3 (ΔR² = +0.02, F-change = 28.13, p < .001). hierarchical sequence therefore supports retaining bmi provides incremental evidence smoking. Default change tokens auto-injected: c("r2_change", "f_change", "p_change") lm (APA hierarchical-regression standard), c("lrt_change", "p_change") glm (Hosmer & Lemeshow §3.5), c("aic_change", "bic_change", "lrt_change", "p_change") mixed-effects fits (lmer / glmer / glmmTMB / nlme::lme — Pinheiro & Bates 2000 §2.4.1). Customise via show_fit_stats; order tokens controls order rows. change tokens available: "adj_r2_change", "f2_change", "deviance_change", "aic_change" / "aicc_change" / "bic_change". Variance-explained change tokens (Δr², Δf²) NA mixed-effects pairs — F-test framework grounds doesn’t apply. Validation strict: identical nobs identical response across models, otherwise spicy_invalid_input error explains listwise-deletion trap suggests refitting common subset.
code: sochealth_cc <- sochealth |>   dplyr::select(wellbeing_score, age, sex, smoking, bmi, region) |>   na.omit() m1 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex,                 data = sochealth_cc) m2 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking,       data = sochealth_cc) m3 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + bmi, data = sochealth_cc) table_regression(list(m1, m2, m3), nested = TRUE) #> Hierarchical linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>                          Model 1                Model 2            Model 3      #>                    ────────────────────  ───────────────────── smoking:        │                                                              #>    No (ref.)     │                           –      –     –         –      –    #>    Yes           │ +0.02         #>  F-change        │     –                   +2.28                 +28.13         #>  p (change)      │     –                     .132                  <.001        #>  #> (ref.) │  –     #>    Male          │ <.001  #>  smoking:        │        #>    No (ref.)     │  –     #>    Yes           │  .119  #>  bmi             │ <.001  #>  #> Note. Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: default vcov = "classical" reports OLS standard error, valid homoskedastic, independent errors. Two robust alternatives first-class arguments; bootstrap jackknife variants also available via vcov = "bootstrap" / "jackknife".

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: error variance plausibly depends predictors (ubiquitous concern cross-sectional social-science data), set vcov = "HC*" sandwich-style standard errors via [sandwich::vcovHC()][sandwich::vcovHC]. valid types HC0 HC5; HC3 small-sample-friendly default (Long Ervin 2000): footer reads “Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3)”; column header confidence interval automatically tracks ci_level.
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking, data = sochealth) table_regression(fit, vcov = "HC3") #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.61  [62.05, 68.35]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.03  [-0.01,  0.11]   .127  #>  sex:            │                                       #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.91  [ 2.07,  5.64]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                       #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.11  [-3.91,  0.47]   .123  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                  #>  R²              │    0.02                               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: clustered observations (repeated measures person, students within schools, observations within regions), vcov = "CR*" requests cluster-robust variance via [clubSandwich::vcovCR()][clubSandwich::vcovCR], cluster identifier supplied cluster. Three forms accepted: Formula — cluster = ~region. variables looked model.frame(fit) first, model’s original data argument. Recommended: independent dataset’s name, composable multi-way clustering (cluster = ~region:year). String — cluster = "region". Single column name resolved way. Convenient express interactions. Vector — cluster = df$region. Atomic vector length nobs(fit). Use cluster key derived fly (cluster = interaction(df$region, df$year)) pulled different dataset matching row order. Bare unquoted names (cluster = region) accepted — require non-standard evaluation breaks programmatic use (function wrapping, loops, dynamic column choice). CR2 (Bell-McCaffrey adjustment) recommended default clusters (Pustejovsky Tipton 2018; Imbens Kolesár 2016). Coefficient inference uses [clubSandwich::coef_test()][clubSandwich::coef_test] Satterthwaite-corrected degrees freedom. AME columns requested, Satterthwaite framework applied AME contrast via clubSandwich::linear_contrast(), B AME share t-distribution df — visible footer.
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking, data = sochealth_cc) table_regression(   fit,   vcov = "CR2",   cluster = ~region,   show_columns = c("b", "se", "ci", "p", "ame", "ame_p") ) #> Registered S3 methods overwritten by 'clubSandwich': #>   method        from     #>   bread.lmerMod merDeriv #>   bread.mlm     sandwich #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p     AME     p    #> ─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.00  1.74  [60.49, 69.51]  <.001                #>  age             │    0.05  0.04  [-0.05,  0.15]   .247   0.05   .247  #>  sex:            │                                                     #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –       –     –     #>    Male          │    3.88  0.85  [ 1.68,  6.07]   .006   3.88   .006  #>  smoking:        │                                                     #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –       –     –     #>    Yes           │   -1.68  1.55  [-5.72,  2.37]   .331  -1.68   .331  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1163                                                #>  R²              │    0.02                                             #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                                             #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: cluster-robust (CR2), clusters by region. #> AME = average marginal effect. #> AME inference: t-test with Satterthwaite df.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: p_adjust applies family-wise false-discovery-rate adjustment p-values via [stats::p.adjust()][stats::p.adjust]. family model’s full coefficient set (intercept reference rows excluded); B AME adjusted independent families within call. Available methods stats::p.adjust(): "none" (default), "holm", "hochberg", "hommel", "bonferroni", "BH" / "fdr", "". footer documents chosen method family size; SE column unchanged. p_adjust orthogonal vcov: combining robust SE family-wise correction fully supported, e.g. vcov = "HC3", p_adjust = "holm". adjustment applied keep / drop filtering, family stays model’s full coefficient set displayed adjusted p-values reflect right denominator regardless subset shown. methodological note. Adjusting p-values every coefficient single regression model standard convention social-science clinical reporting (Rothman 1990; Gelman, Hill, Yajima 2012; Greenland 2017; Harrell 2015 §5.4; APA Manual 7 §6.46). coefficient tests scientifically distinct hypothesis distinct predictor, situation family-wise procedures designed . default "none" reflects consensus. Adjustment nonetheless legitimate three contexts: Mass screening many candidate predictors prior hypothesis — typically "BH" (Benjamini–Hochberg, false discovery rate). Pre-registered multi-endpoint confirmatory designs — typically "holm" (Holm’s step-, strong family-wise error rate control). reviewer, editor, statistical analysis plan explicitly requests — apply requested method document footer. argument follows transparency rejection rule standardized: method available, choice among methods analyst’s, footer makes choice visible reader.
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + education,           data = sh) table_regression(fit, p_adjust = "bonferroni") #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable                 │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │   53.96  1.66  [50.70, 57.22]  <.001  #>  age                      │    0.03  0.03  [-0.03,  0.08]  1.000  #>  sex:                     │                                       #>    Female (ref.)          │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male                   │    3.57  0.81  [ 1.99,  5.15]  <.001  #>  smoking:                 │                                       #>    No (ref.)              │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes                    │    0.68  0.99  [-1.27,  2.63]  1.000  #>  education:               │                                       #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Upper secondary        │   11.89  1.05  [ 9.82, 13.96]  <.001  #>    Tertiary               │   19.71  1.12  [17.51, 21.91]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                        │ 1175                                  #>  R²                       │    0.22                               #>  Adj.R²                   │    0.22                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> P-values adjusted via stats::p.adjust(method = "bonferroni"); m = 5 coefficient(s) per model.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: keep drop accept regular expressions matched coefficient names (returned [stats::coef()]). mutually exclusive — use keep retain focal predictors, drop hide control variables. Multiple patterns combine logical . Together p_adjust, article-ready workflow: adjust full model, display rows reader cares — table_regression(fit, p_adjust = "BH", keep = "^treatment").

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: Stars default. APA 7 §6.46 explicitly discourages asterisks-reporting favour exact p-values, ASA’s post-2019 guidance (Wasserstein, Schirm, Lazar 2019) reinforces point. Set stars = TRUE APA preset (*** p < .001, ** p < .01, * p < .05) pass named numeric vector custom thresholds: Stars suffix B column (β standardisation requested); threshold mapping auto-documented footer. p column remains unstarred numeric value stays readable.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: labels accepts named character vector keyed either formula term labels ("sex") coefficient names ("sexMale"), individual contrast rows can relabelled. intercept_position switches intercept bottom (Stata convention). reference_style = "annotation" lifts reference level factor header ("sex: [ref: Female]") drops explicit reference row, compact tables. decimal_mark = "," switches European decimal notation automatically changes CI separator ";" avoid ambiguity:
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + education, data = sh) table_regression(   fit,   labels = c(     age            = "Age (years)",     sex            = "Sex",     education      = "Education"   ),   reference_style = "annotation",   intercept_position = "last",   decimal_mark = "," ) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable                          │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  Age (years)                       │    0,03  0,03  [-0,03;  0,08]   ,343  #>  Sex: [ref: Female]                │                                       #>    Male                            │    3,65  0,80  [ 2,09;  5,22]  <,001  #>  Education: [ref: Lower secondary] │                                       #>    Upper secondary                 │   11,85  1,04  [ 9,81; 13,89]  <,001  #>    Tertiary                        │   19,52  1,10  [17,36; 21,67]  <,001  #>  (Intercept)                       │   54,18  1,63  [50,99; 57,37]  <,001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                                 │ 1200                                  #>  R²                                │    0,22                               #>  Adj.R²                            │    0,22                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: table_regression() accepts glm() fit. Inference defaults z-asymptotic Wald regime — convention summary.glm() applies fixed-dispersion families (binomial, Poisson), shared Stata’s logit, SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION. One caveat cross-checking: families estimated dispersion (gaussian, Gamma, inverse.gaussian, quasi- families), summary.glm() switches t statistics residual degrees freedom, p-values run larger table’s z-based ones small samples — table follows Stata’s glm default rather summary.glm(). table title becomes family-aware (“Logistic regression”, “Poisson regression”, “Probit regression”, …) default fit-statistics block swaps nobs, pseudo_r2_mcfadden, pseudo_r2_nagelkerke, AIC instead R² Adj.R². illustrate logistic regression smoking sex, age, education sochealth, reusing sh — copy sochealth education plain (unordered) factor, introduced Per-coefficient effect sizes — contrasts dummy-coded, one row per level. model mixes binary factor (sex), numeric predictor (age), 3-level factor (education) — enough exercise every glm-specific feature . Read fit block care. McFadden Nagelkerke R² likelihood-based analogues R², proportions explained variance — values comparable OLS R², different measures disagree construction fit (0.02 McFadden versus 0.03 Nagelkerke). Long Freese (2014, §3.3) find scalar measures limited utility except rough index comparing competing models data, prefer information criteria — hence AIC default block. reporting one, name specific measure, row labels ; generic “pseudo-R²” ambiguous across software. Tjur’s (2009) coefficient discrimination, alternative probability scale binary outcomes, available opt-token pseudo_r2_tjur via show_fit_stats.
code: fit <- glm(smoking ~ sex + age + education, data = sh,            family = binomial) table_regression(fit) #> Logistic regression: smoking #>  #>  Variable                 │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │   -1.11  0.29  [-1.67, -0.55]  <.001  #>  sex:                     │                                       #>    Female (ref.)          │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male                   │   -0.04  0.14  [-0.32,  0.25]   .800  #>  age                      │    0.01  0.00  [-0.00,  0.02]   .214  #>  education:               │                                       #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Upper secondary        │   -0.48  0.17  [-0.82, -0.14]   .005  #>    Tertiary               │   -0.91  0.20  [-1.29, -0.52]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                        │ 1175                                  #>  R² (McFadden)            │    0.02                               #>  R² (Nagelkerke)          │    0.03                               #>  AIC                      │ 1200.9                                #>  #> Note. Logistic regression. #> Std. errors: classical (Fisher information).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: Set exponentiate = TRUE switch B column ratio scale relabel header according family link: binomial(logit), IRR poisson(log), HR binomial(cloglog), RR binomial(log), MR Gamma(log), generic exp(B) log-link families (genuine ratio means). Links whose exponential ratio — probit, cauchit, inverse (Gamma() default), among others — refused clear error rather silently mislabelled; report response-scale effects models via AME column instead. standard error follows delta-method approximation SE_OR = × SE_log-odds (Stata logit, convention). test statistic p-value stay link scale, B = 0 = 1 hypothesis Wald approximation accurate, match unexponentiated table verbatim. (Wald tests invariant reparameterisation — z rebuilt SE_OR give different statistic p-value, link-scale test kept; Long & Freese 2014 §3.2.2.): Read exponentiated rows factor-change template (Long Freese 2014 §6.1.1): Tertiary, = 0.40, 95% CI [0.27, 0.59] — holding sex age constant, odds smoking tertiary-educated respondent 0.40 times odds one lower secondary education, 60% decrease odds (percentage change = 100 × (− 1)). odds ratio ratio odds, probabilities: smoking prevalence around 21% sits farther 1 corresponding risk ratio, reading “60% lower probability smoking” overstates effect. Average marginal effects section reports probability-scale counterpart contrast (−0.15, , 15 percentage points).
code: table_regression(fit, exponentiate = TRUE) #> Logistic regression: smoking #>  #>  Variable                 │   OR      SE      95% CI       p    #> ──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │    0.33  0.09  [0.19, 0.58]  <.001  #>  sex:                     │                                     #>    Female (ref.)          │     –     –         –         –     #>    Male                   │    0.96  0.14  [0.73, 1.28]   .800  #>  age                      │    1.01  0.00  [1.00, 1.02]   .214  #>  education:               │                                     #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │     –     –         –         –     #>    Upper secondary        │    0.62  0.11  [0.44, 0.87]   .005  #>    Tertiary               │    0.40  0.08  [0.27, 0.59]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                        │ 1175                                #>  R² (McFadden)            │    0.02                             #>  R² (Nagelkerke)          │    0.03                             #>  AIC                      │ 1200.9                              #>  #> Note. Logistic regression. #> Std. errors: classical (Fisher information). #> OR = odds ratio. #> Coefficients exponentiated and displayed as OR; SE on the OR scale (delta method); CI bounds exponentiated (asymmetric).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: partial_chi2 glm analog partial_f2: model term, partial likelihood-ratio chi-square via drop1(test = "LRT") (SAS PROC GENMOD TYPE3 likelihood-ratio analysis; Long & Freese 2014 §3.2.2, §3.2.4). Rendered value (df) factor terms (k − 1 df) numeric terms (1 df) read glance: p column χ² column answer different questions. per-level p-value compares one education level reference, changes reference level changes; joint test — 21.70 2 df, p < .001 — asks whether education whole improves model, invariant reference choice. factor’s levels straddle significance, joint test one report. Wald likelihood-ratio tests joint hypothesis asymptotically equivalent differ finite samples; many statisticians prefer likelihood-ratio form available (Long & Freese 2014 §3.2.2), partial_chi2 computes.
code: fit2 <- glm(smoking ~ age + education, data = sh, family = binomial) table_regression(fit2, show_columns = c("b", "partial_chi2", "p")) #> Logistic regression: smoking #>  #>  Variable                 │    B        χ²        p    #> ──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │   -1.13             <.001  #>  age                      │    0.01   1.55 (1)   .213  #>  education:               │                            #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │     –                –     #>    Upper secondary        │   -0.48  21.70 (2)   .005  #>    Tertiary               │   -0.91  21.70 (2)  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                        │ 1175                       #>  R² (McFadden)            │    0.02                    #>  R² (Nagelkerke)          │    0.03                    #>  AIC                      │ 1198.9                     #>  #> Note. Logistic regression. #> Std. errors: classical (Fisher information). #> χ² = partial likelihood-ratio chi-squared.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: glm, standardized = "refit" z-scores numeric predictors refits model — response stays observed scale link function fixed. “x-standardization” convention (Long Freese 2014 §4.7.2). algebraic methods ("posthoc", "basic", "smart") apply X-scaling using algebra lm case. standardized = "pseudo" (glm ) Menard (2004, 2011) fully standardised coefficient, scaling SD(X) / SD(Y*) Y* latent variable link scale SD(Y*) = sqrt(var(linear-predictor) + var_link) var_link = π²/3 logit, 1 probit, π²/6 cloglog. Defined binomial families; non-binomial returns NA spicy_caveat:

what: Average marginal effects: probability units, not log-odds
title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: glm, AME averaged observed sample — partial derivative numeric predictors, discrete change factor levels — response scale, link scale. logistic regression means displayed AME probability units, log-odds: AME -0.15 education = Tertiary reads “average, holding sex age constant, respondent tertiary education 15 percentage points less likely current smoker one lower secondary education”. almost always quantity substantive reader wants logistic regression, reason AME increasingly recommended odds ratios communicating logistic effects (Mood 2010; Long Freese 2014 §6.2.3). Point estimate inference delegated [marginaleffects::avg_slopes()][marginaleffects::avg_slopes]. cluster-robust variance, Satterthwaite-df handling described Robust variance section applies glm AME well.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: default ci_method = "wald" (symmetric estimate ± z × SE, matching summary.glm). glm can also request ci_method = "profile", calls MASS::confint.glm() compute profile-likelihood CI — asymmetric, obtained inverting likelihood-ratio statistic rather quadratic Wald approximation, preferred choice sparse data near-boundary estimates (Venables & Ripley MASS §7.2). estimate, SE, statistic, p-value remain Wald; "profile" refines CI bounds:

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: nested = TRUE list nested glm fits appends Δχ² p (change) rows fit-statistics block — default change tokens c("lrt_change", "p_change"), Hierarchical / nested regression — APA-conventional layout hierarchical logistic regression (Hosmer & Lemeshow §3.5; Long & Freese 2014 §3.2.4). chi-square statistic comes anova(test = "LRT"). variance-explained change tokens (r2_change, f_change) defined glm — requesting show_fit_stats raises spicy_invalid_input error points pseudo-R² family lrt_change + p_change instead:
code: m1 <- glm(smoking ~ sex,                   data = sh, family = binomial) m2 <- glm(smoking ~ sex + age,             data = sh, family = binomial) m3 <- glm(smoking ~ sex + age + education, data = sh, family = binomial) table_regression(list(m1, m2, m3), nested = TRUE) #> Hierarchical logistic regression: smoking #>  #>                                   Model 1                Model 2         #>                             ────────────────────  ─────────────────────  #>  Variable                 │    B      SE     p       B       SE     p    #> ──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)              │   -1.29  0.10  <.001    -1.54   0.26  <.001  #>  sex:                     │                                              #>    Female (ref.)          │     –     –     –         –      –     –     #>    Male                   │   -0.05  0.14   .713    -0.05   0.14   .722  #>  age                      │                          0.01   0.00   .294  #>  education:               │                                              #>    Lower secondary (ref.) │                                              #>    Upper secondary        │                                              #>    Tertiary               │                                              #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                        │ 1175                  1175                   #>  R² (McFadden)            │    0.00 #>  sex:                     │                        #>    Female (ref.)          │     –      –     –     #>    Male                   │   -0.04   0.14   .800  #>  age                      │    0.01 (Nagelkerke)          │    0.03                #>  AIC                      │ 1200.9                 #>  Δχ²                      │  +21.64                #>  p (change)               │    <.001 Logistic regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (Fisher information).

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: glm() family = gaussian link = "identity" mathematically equivalent lm() lacks variance- explained effect-size family (partial_f2 / η² / ω²) Satterthwaite-corrected AME path. Following transparency rejection rule, spicy accepts fit emits spicy_caveat suggesting refit lm().

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: table_regression() supports four mixed-effects engines: lme4::lmer() lme4::glmer(), glmmTMB::glmmTMB(), nlme::lme(). fixed-effects section table follows conventions lm / glm. , subordinate Random effects block reports variance components rows — one per random standard deviation (σ), correlation (ρ), residual — estimate, SE, CI shared coefficient columns. number groups — one N (<grouping factor>) row per grouping factor, N (Subject) — Nakagawa marginal / conditional R² (Nakagawa & Schielzeth 2013) join fit-statistic rows, align per model comparison table: ICC row appears : random-slope fit single intra-class correlation, row dropped automatically (intercept-case shows ). Two deliberate conventions: variance-component rows carry p-value. null hypothesis σ = 0 sits boundary parameter space, Wald test invalid (Self & Liang 1987); reporting guideline requests per-component p, R ecosystem (lme4, nlme, parameters, broom.mixed), Stata, MLwiN omit . correct significance signal random part footer’s likelihood-ratio test -random-effects model, whose p-value applies boundary correction. single variance component halving p = 0.5 × P(χ²₁ > LR) exact Self & Liang (1987) 50:50 mixture — chibar2(01) line Stata’s mixed prints. random parameters, (two variances correlation), exact reference multi-component mixture (Stram & Lee 1994); spicy reports pragmatic halved χ²_q, less conservative plain χ²_q Stata prints joint tests caveat conservative. footer also carries estimator label — (REML) restricted maximum likelihood (lmer / lme default) (ML) full ML (glmer, glmmTMB default, lmer(..., REML = FALSE)). label informational: tells reader likelihood variance estimates anchored , without implying REML ML estimates interchangeable inference. display inference controls arguments, conventions: show_re = FALSE hides block, re_scale = "variance" switches rows σ σ², re_columns restricts quantities variance rows show, re_ci = "profile" replaces Wald CIs variance components boundary-respecting profile-likelihood CIs (Wald default profiling far slower), re_test = "lrt" "rlrt" adds opt-per-component test (“need random slope?”) boundary correction applied. footer names fixed-effect inference method per engine — Satterthwaite t lmerTest loaded, Wald-z glmer / glmmTMB plain lmer, containment-df t nlme::lme. AME tokens show_columns (see Average marginal effects ) work identically mixed-effects fits — always response scale, Wald-z inference. nested = TRUE works mixed-effects fits change tokens listed Hierarchical / nested regression; note plain-χ² reference appropriate steps add fixed effects, random-structure steps belong re_test, whose reference respects boundary. model-building sequence, REML/ML estimator choice, Nakagawa R² ICC mechanics, per-class inference paths, singular fits, testing random components, glmmTMB / nlme::lme specifics — including worked glmer AME example — see vignette("table-regression-mixed", package = "spicy").
code: library(lme4) #> Loading required package: Matrix fit <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy) table_regression(fit) #> Linear mixed-effects regression: Reaction #>  #>  Variable                        │    B      SE        95% CI         p    #> ─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)                     │  251.41  6.82  [238.03, 264.78]  <.001  #>  Days                            │   10.47  1.55  [  7.44,  13.50]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  Random effects:                 │                                         #>    σ Subject (Intercept)         │   24.74  5.84  [  6.79,  34.32]   –     #>    σ Subject Days                │    5.92  1.25  [  2.47,   8.00]   –     #>    ρ Subject ((Intercept), Days) │    0.07  0.33  [ -0.57,   0.70]   –     #>    σ (Residual)                  │   25.59  1.51  [ 22.44,  28.39]   –     #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                               │  180                                    #>  N (Subject)                     │   18                                    #>  R² (marginal)                   │    0.28                                 #>  R² (conditional)                │    0.80                                 #>  AIC                             │ 1755.6                                  #>  BIC                             │ 1774.8                                  #>  #> Note. Linear mixed-effects regression. #> Std. errors: Wald (model-based). #> p-values: Wald-z, large-sample approximation. Load `lmerTest` for Satterthwaite t-tests. #> Random effects (REML): LR test vs linear regression, χ̄²(3) = 150.04, p < .001.

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text:
default print method draws ASCII table console. content available plain data.frame (output = "data.frame"), long broom-style data.frame (output = "long"), rich-format table gt, tinytable, flextable, excel, word, clipboard: #mwlefoodfi table { font-family: system-ui, ‘Segoe UI’, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, ‘Apple Color Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Symbol’, ‘Noto Color Emoji’; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale; }

#mwlefoodfi thead, #mwlefoodfi tbody, #mwlefoodfi tfoot, #mwlefoodfi tr, #mwlefoodfi td, #mwlefoodfi th { border-style: none; }

#mwlefoodfi p { margin: 0; padding: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_table { display: table; border-collapse: collapse; line-height: normal; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; color: #333333; font-size: 16px; font-weight: normal; font-style: normal; background-color: #FFFFFF; width: auto; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #A8A8A8; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #A8A8A8; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_caption { padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_title { color: #333333; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-width: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_subtitle { color: #333333; font-size: 85%; font-weight: initial; padding-top: 3px; padding-bottom: 5px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-top-color: #FFFFFF; border-top-width: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_heading { background-color: #FFFFFF; text-align: center; border-bottom-color: #FFFFFF; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_bottom_border { border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_col_headings { border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #333333; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_col_heading { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; text-transform: inherit; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: bottom; padding-top: 5px; padding-bottom: 6px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; overflow-x: hidden; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner_outer { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: normal; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 0; padding-bottom: 0; padding-left: 4px; padding-right: 4px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner_outer:first-child { padding-left: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner_outer:last-child { padding-right: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_column_spanner { border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #333333; vertical-align: bottom; padding-top: 5px; padding-bottom: 5px; overflow-x: hidden; display: inline-block; width: 100%; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_spanner_row { border-bottom-style: hidden; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_group_heading { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 2px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; text-align: left; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_empty_group_heading { padding: 0.5px; color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 2px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_from_md > :first-child { margin-top: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_from_md > :last-child { margin-bottom: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; margin: 10px; border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 1px; border-top-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0); border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 1px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 1px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; vertical-align: middle; overflow-x: hidden; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_stub { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-right-style: solid; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_stub_row_group { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; font-size: 100%; font-weight: initial; text-transform: inherit; border-right-style: solid; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; vertical-align: top; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_row_group_first td { border-top-width: 2px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_row_group_first th { border-top-width: 2px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_summary_row { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_first_summary_row { border-top-style: solid; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_first_summary_row.thick { border-top-width: 2px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_last_summary_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_grand_summary_row { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; text-transform: inherit; padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_first_grand_summary_row { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-top-style: double; border-top-width: 6px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_last_grand_summary_row_top { padding-top: 8px; padding-bottom: 8px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; border-bottom-style: double; border-bottom-width: 6px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_striped { background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_table_body { border-top-style: solid; border-top-width: 0px; border-top-color: #D3D3D3; border-bottom-style: solid; border-bottom-width: 0px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_footnotes { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-style: none; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_footnote { margin: 0px; font-size: 90%; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_sourcenotes { color: #333333; background-color: #FFFFFF; border-bottom-style: none; border-bottom-width: 2px; border-bottom-color: #D3D3D3; border-left-style: none; border-left-width: 2px; border-left-color: #D3D3D3; border-right-style: none; border-right-width: 2px; border-right-color: #D3D3D3; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_sourcenote { font-size: 90%; padding-top: 4px; padding-bottom: 4px; padding-left: 5px; padding-right: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_left { text-align: left; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_center { text-align: center; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_right { text-align: right; font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_font_normal { font-weight: normal; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_font_bold { font-weight: bold; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_font_italic { font-style: italic; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_super { font-size: 65%; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_footnote_marks { font-size: 75%; vertical-align: 0.4em; position: initial; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_asterisk { font-size: 100%; vertical-align: 0; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_1 { text-indent: 5px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_2 { text-indent: 10px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_3 { text-indent: 15px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_4 { text-indent: 20px; }

#mwlefoodfi .gt_indent_5 { text-indent: 25px; }

#mwlefoodfi .katex-display { display: inline-flex !important; margin-bottom: 0.75em !important; }

#mwlefoodfi div.Reactable > div.rt-table > div.rt-thead > div.rt-tr.rt-tr-group-header > div.rt-th-group:{ height: 0px !important; }

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: broom::tidy() returns long tibble one row per (model_id, term, estimate_type) broom-canonical column names (estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, statistic, p.value). broom::glance() returns one row per (model_id, outcome) model-level statistics; df.residual preserved numeric cluster-robust Satterthwaite df flows verbatim: long format right entry point table one step larger pipeline — saving disk manuscript appendix, faceting subgroup, feeding downstream post-estimation analysis. tidy() output keeps estimate_type column data frame can hold rows B, β, AME, per-coefficient effect-size estimates without ambiguity.
code: fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking, data = sochealth) out <- table_regression(fit, standardized = "refit")  broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 8 × 16 #>   model_id outcome outcome_level term  estimate_type estimate std.error conf.low #>   <chr>    <chr>   <chr>         <chr> <chr>            <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 M1       wellbe… NA            (Int… B              65.2       1.66    62.0    #> 2 M1       wellbe… NA            (Int… beta           -0.0961    0.0431  -0.181  #> 3 M1       wellbe… NA            age   B               0.0465    0.0307  -0.0137 #> 4 M1       wellbe… NA            age   beta            0.0439    0.0290  -0.0130 #> 5 M1       wellbe… NA            sexM… B               3.86      0.905    2.08   #> 6 M1       wellbe… NA            sexM… beta            0.247     0.0579   0.133  #> 7 M1       wellbe… NA            smok… B              -1.72      1.11    -3.89   #> 8 M1       wellbe… NA            smok… beta           -0.110     0.0708  -0.249  #> # ℹ 8 more variables: conf.high <dbl>, statistic <dbl>, df <dbl>, #> #   p.value <dbl>, test_type <chr>, is_intercept <lgl>, factor_term <chr>, #> #   factor_level <chr> broom::glance(out) #> # A tibble: 1 × 15 #>   model_id outcome       nobs weighted_nobs r.squared adj.r.squared omega2 sigma #>   <chr>    <chr>        <int>         <dbl>     <dbl>         <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 M1       wellbeing_s…  1175            NA    0.0190        0.0165 0.0165  15.5 #> # ℹ 7 more variables: rmse <dbl>, f2 <dbl>, AIC <dbl>, AICc <dbl>, BIC <dbl>, #> #   deviance <dbl>, df.residual <dbl>

title: Publication-ready regression tables
text: vignette("categorical-predictors", package = "spicy") categorical predictors across model families: dummy coding reference levels, joint tests, ordinal predictors (scores vs dummies), categorization trap. Model-family vignettes build mechanics shown : vignette("table-regression-mixed", package = "spicy") mixed-effects (multilevel) models: random effects table rows, ICC, boundary-corrected chi-bar-squared test, opt-per-term LRT / RLRT. vignette("table-regression-counts", package = "spicy") count two-part models: Poisson negative-binomial rate ratios, offsets, zero-inflated / hurdle components labelled blocks. vignette("table-regression-ordinal", package = "spicy") ordinal (proportional-odds) models: shared slopes, ordered thresholds rows, per-category marginal effects. vignette("table-regression-multinomial", package = "spicy") multinomial logistic models: outcome categories columns changing reference category. vignette("table-regression-survival", package = "spicy") survival models: Cox hazard ratios events concordance, accelerated failure time models, survival estimands. Descriptive reporting companions: vignette("table-categorical", package = "spicy") APA Table 1 categorical descriptors (factors, labelled variables, chi-squared tests, association measures). vignette("table-continuous", package = "spicy") APA Table 1 / Table 2 continuous descriptors unadjusted group-comparison tests. vignette("table-continuous-lm", package = "spicy") one-predictor--many-outcomes linear-model counterpart (estimated marginal means, contrast slope, four effect-size families noncentral CIs). vignette("summary-tables-reporting", package = "spicy") end--end reporting workflow combines four spicy summary-table helpers along APA Table 1 / 2 / 3 sequence.

title: Explore variables and build codebooks
text: Variable inspection helps catch common problems early: unclear names, missing labels, unexpected coding, variables many missing values. quick review dataset also makes easier choose variables tabulate, summarize, report later.

what: Recover labels from imported column names
title: Explore variables and build codebooks
text: imported files store variable name variable label column header. label_from_names() splits names form name<sep>label, renames columns, stores label proper variable label. default separator ". " chosen match LimeSurvey CSV exports taken Export results -> Export format: CSV -> Headings: Question code & question text, produce column names form "code. question text". Pass sep = use literal separator.
code: df <- tibble::tibble(   "age. Age of respondent" = c(25, 30, 41),   "edu. Highest education level" = c("Lower", "Upper", "Tertiary"),   "smoke. Current smoker" = c("No", "Yes", "No") )  out <- label_from_names(df) labelled::var_label(out) #> $age #> [1] "Age of respondent" #>  #> $edu #> [1] "Highest education level" #>  #> $smoke #> [1] "Current smoker"

title: Explore variables and build codebooks
text: varlist() gives compact summary variable, including name, label, representative values, class, number distinct values, number valid observations, missing values. RStudio Positron, main way use varlist() interactively. default behavior, opens searchable, sortable variable overview Viewer, makes easy scan labels, look specific variables, filter want inspect, review structure dataset analysis. prefer shorter call interactive work, vl() shortcut varlist(): want summary returned tibble, use tbl = TRUE: want Values column include explicit missing values, use include_na = TRUE: want display unique non-missing values Values column, use values = TRUE. especially useful variables small number distinct values: focused inspection, select variables want review: often enough confirm labels, factor levels, missing values look correct moving tabulations.
