title: Explore variables and build codebooks
text: want searchable exportable overview whole dataset selected set variables, code_book() builds interactive codebook Viewer. Use tidyselect-style selectors varlist() build focused codebook: can also request fuller display values include missing values explicitly summary: useful reviewing dataset collaborators preparing documentation analysis.
code: if (requireNamespace("DT", quietly = TRUE)) {   code_book(sochealth) } if (requireNamespace("DT", quietly = TRUE)) {   code_book(     sochealth,     starts_with("bmi"),     values = TRUE,     title = "BMI codebook",     filename = "bmi_codebook"   ) } if (requireNamespace("DT", quietly = TRUE)) {   code_book(sochealth, values = TRUE, include_na = TRUE) }

what: When to use varlist() and code_book()
title: Explore variables and build codebooks
text: Use varlist() want quick summary script tibble can inspect directly. Use vl() want summary shorter call interactive work. Use code_book() want searchable, interactive codebook review export. two tools share column structure differ one default: varlist() shows observed factor levels Values column (Stata tab style), whereas code_book() shows declared levels including unused ones (SPSS FREQUENCIES style, appropriate schema documentation). Pass factor_levels = explicitly either function override default.

what: spicy: APA-style regression and summary tables for R
title: spicy: APA-style regression and summary tables for R
text: spicy R package frequency tables, cross-tabulations, association measures, categorical continuous summary tables, publication-ready regression tables 30+ model classes, labelled survey data workflows.

title: spicy: APA-style regression and summary tables for R
text: Every spicy table prints readable text console exports gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard, following APA conventions. Around tables, spicy provides survey-data tools feed : variable inspection, codebooks, label extraction, row-wise summaries. Frequency tables freq(). Cross-tabulations cross_tab(): percentages, weights, chi-squared tests, effect sizes. Association measures: cramer_v(), phi(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), somers_d() 6 coefficients; assoc_measures() computes full set . Categorical continuous summary tables table_categorical() table_continuous(), overall group. Model-based continuous summary tables table_continuous_lm() linear-model reporting: classical / HC* / cluster-robust / bootstrap / jackknife variance, four effect-size families (f², Cohen’s d, Hedges’ g, Hays’ omega²) noncentral CIs, additive covariate adjustment G-computation (Stata margins style) equal-weight (emmeans style) marginal means, weighted comparisons. Regression tables table_regression() one fitted models side side, across 30+ model classes (see Supported models ): classical / heteroskedasticity-robust / cluster-robust / bootstrap / jackknife variance class’s field-standard backend, standardised coefficients, family-aware exponentiate (/ IRR / HR / RR / MR, link-gated), Wald profile-likelihood CIs, average marginal effects (per-category ordinal multinomial models), partial f² / η² / ω² / χ² effect sizes, class-aware fit statistics (pseudo-R², Nakagawa marginal / conditional R², ICC), hierarchical model comparisons correct nested test per class, multiple-comparison adjustment. Mixed models report random effects table rows optional boundary-correct per-term test; ordinal models report thresholds; zero-inflated hurdle models report every model component. Variable inspection varlist() vl(): names, labels, values, classes, missing data. Codebooks code_book(): interactive exportable, labelled survey-style datasets. Label extraction label_from_names(), including LimeSurvey-style headers. Row-wise summaries mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n(), explicit control missing values. Works labelled, factor, ordered, Date, POSIXct, common variable types. full introduction, see Getting started spicy.

title: spicy: APA-style regression and summary tables for R
text: table_regression() accepts single fit list fits classes, renders conventions model family: Class-specific structure renders labelled blocks table: random effects (SE CI variance component, optional boundary-correct per-term likelihood-ratio test), ordinal thresholds, non-proportional effects, zero-inflation dispersion components. Robust variance requests use class’s field-standard backend refused clear message valid backend exists.

title: Coerce a spicy_categorical_table to a plain data frame or tibble — as.data.frame.spicy_categorical_table
text: S3 methods strip "spicy_categorical_table" / "spicy_table" classes rendering-attributes (display_df, indent_text, align, decimal_mark, long_data, ...) object returned table_categorical() underlying wide-format data can manipulated downstream tools (dplyr, tidyr, etc.) standard data.frame / tbl_df contract. single attribute "group_var" preserved lightweight provenance marker; spicy attributes dropped. original x unaffected, print(x) continues render formatted ASCII table.

title: Extract the typed (structured) view of a spicy_regression_table — as_structured
text: table_regression() returns display representation default – character data.frame stars suffixes, em-dash reference rows, bracketed "[L, U]" confidence intervals, APA padding p-values. accessor returns typed view output engines (Excel, gt, tinytable, flextable, clipboard) consume internally: fully numeric body CI pre-split LL / UL columns, NAs non-applicable / reference cells, plus per-cell markers format specification.

code: as_structured(x)

text: x spicy_regression_table object returned table_regression().

title: Extract the typed (structured) view of a spicy_regression_table — as_structured
text: list structured view (see Details schema).

title: Extract the typed (structured) view of a spicy_regression_table — as_structured
text: right entry point users want : Filter coefficients programmatically, e.g. as_structured(tbl)$body[as_structured(tbl)$body$p < 0.05, ]. Aggregate raw values across rows, e.g. mean(as_structured(tbl)$body[["B"]], na.rm = TRUE). Build custom downstream renderer consumes structured contract spicy's built-engines.

title: Extract the typed (structured) view of a spicy_regression_table — as_structured
text: body – data.frame Variable character column one numeric columns. Confidence intervals split LL / UL columns named like "95% CI: LL" / "95% CI: UL" (prefixed model label multi-model output). Cells value (reference levels, non-applicable rows multi-model output, factor headers) NA. reference_rows, factor_header_rows, fit_stat_rows, level_rows, outcome_row – integer row indices. reference_models_by_row – reference row (keyed row index character string), model_ids models actually contain factor: renderers show reference marker models' columns leave others blank. outcome_labels_by_col – outcome_row (explicit outcome_labels two models), display label keyed model's first structured column name. col_meta – per-column metadata keyed structured column name (token, model_id, precision, p-style, -threshold, CI pair / role / label). spanners – named list mapping model labels column indices body (multi-model ). ci_pairs – list (label, cols) entries describing CI pair body. format_spec – global format defaults (decimal mark, digits, p-style, CI level, etc.).

title: Extract the typed (structured) view of a spicy_regression_table — as_structured

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: assoc_measures() computes range association measures two-way contingency table returns tidy data frame.

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
code: assoc_measures(   x,   type = c("all", "nominal", "ordinal"),   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L )

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: x contingency table (class table). type family measures compute: "" (default), "nominal", "ordinal". conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3).

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: data frame columns measure, estimate, se, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value. p_value comes two test families: Pearson chi-squared test independence Cramer's V, Phi, Contingency Coefficient (three chi-squared-derived nominal measures). three carry chi-squared p-value given table. Wald z-test H0: measure = 0 every measure: Yule's Q, Lambda, Goodman-Kruskal's Tau, Uncertainty Coefficient, ordinal measures (Gamma, Tau-b, Tau-c, Somers' D). Direction-dependent measures (lambda_gk(), goodman_kruskal_tau(), uncertainty_coef(), somers_d()) contribute one row per direction (symmetric / R|C / C|R applicable), output rows number helper functions.

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: type = "" (default) returns nominal ordinal measures. Use type = "nominal" type = "ordinal" restrict output single family. nominal family includes cramer_v(), contingency_coef(), lambda_gk(), goodman_kruskal_tau(), uncertainty_coef(), (2x2 tables) phi() yule_q(). ordinal family includes gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), kendall_tau_c(), somers_d(). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024).

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: Agresti, . (2002). Categorical Data Analysis (2nd ed.). Wiley. Liebetrau, . M. (1983). Measures Association. Sage. Signorell, . et al. (2024). DescTools: Tools Descriptive Statistics. R package.

what: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: Low-level rendering engine constructs visually aligned ASCII table data.frame. Supports Unicode line-drawing characters, ANSI colors via crayon, automatic colored-text-aware width detection, configurable padding, per-column alignment.

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
code: build_ascii_table(   x,   padding = 2L,   first_column_line = TRUE,   row_total_line = TRUE,   column_total_line = TRUE,   bottom_line = FALSE,   lines_color = "darkgrey",   align_left_cols = c(1L, 2L),   align_center_cols = integer(0),   center_headers = FALSE,   spanners = NULL,   group_sep_rows = integer(0),   total_row_idx = NULL,   display_labels = NULL,   ... )

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: x data.frame spicy_table object containing table format. Typically, includes columns Category, Values, Freq., Percent, etc. padding Non-negative integer giving number extra characters added column's auto-computed width (maximum cell-content width header width). Defaults 2L, gives Stata- / cli-like compact look. cell additionally receives one-space gutter side, padding = 2L column whose content 5 characters wide occupies 9 characters total (1 + 5 + 2 + 1). string choices "compact", "normal" "wide" spicy < 0.11.0 removed; pass 0L, 2L 4L instead. Passing string raises actionable error. first_column_line Logical. TRUE (default), vertical separator drawn first column (useful separating categories data). row_total_line, column_total_line Logical. Control horizontal rules total rows columns. default TRUE. bottom_line Logical. FALSE (default), closing line drawn. TRUE, draws closing line bottom table. lines_color Character. Color used table separators. Defaults "darkgrey". color applied ANSI color support available (see crayon::has_color()). align_left_cols Integer vector column indices left-align. Defaults c(1L, 2L) (layout used freq()-style tables); pass explicit vector layouts. align_center_cols Integer vector column indices center-align. Defaults integer(0) (centered columns). Columns align_left_cols align_center_cols right-aligned. center_headers Logical. TRUE, column headers centered column content even data right-aligned (publication convention coefficient / summary tables; matches Stata regress SPSS REGRESSION output). Left-aligned columns (per align_left_cols) keep header left. Defaults FALSE backward compatibility; print.spicy_regression_table method enables . spanners Optional named list defining column group row drawn column headers ("spanner" / "supra-header" convention; APA Manual 7 §7.13). Names spanner labels; values integer vectors 1-based column indices label spans (must contiguous). thin underline rule drawn spanner across span. Used print.spicy_regression_table() display model name model's block sub-columns. Defaults NULL (spanner row). group_sep_rows Integer vector row indices light dashed separator line drawn. Defaults integer(0). total_row_idx Optional integer vector 1-based row indices identifying totals rows; horizontal rule drawn just . NULL (default), falls back regex match "Total" / "Column_Total" formatted row text, can mis-fire user category literally named "Total" "Sub-Total". Cross-tabs frequency tables built cross_tab() freq() set attribute result print methods immune false positive. display_labels Optional character vector length ncol(x) used override colnames(x) rendered header row . data.frame's actual names kept indexing; visual header swapped. Used print.spicy_regression_table() B + AME table shows bare label (95% CI, p) blocks rather R's deduplicated 95% CI.2 / p.2. Defaults NULL (use colnames(x) verbatim). ... Additional arguments (currently ignored).

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: single character string containing full ASCII-formatted table, suitable direct printing cat().

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: users call directly: wrapped spicy_print_table() internal print.spicy_* methods, add titles, notes, table-type-aware alignment defaults. Reach build_ascii_table() need render arbitrary data.frame string look spicy's tables.

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
code: # Internal usage example (for developers) df <- data.frame(   Category = c("Valid", "", "Missing", "Total"),   Values = c("Yes", "No", "NA", ""),   Freq. = c(12, 8, 1, 21),   Percent = c(57.1, 38.1, 4.8, 100.0) )  cat(build_ascii_table(df, padding = 0L)) #>  Category │ Values  Freq.  Percent  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Yes        12     57.1  #>           │ No          8     38.1  #>  Missing  │ NA          1      4.8  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────── #>  Total    │            21      100

what: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: code_book() creates interactive exportable codebook summarizing selected variables data frame. builds upon varlist() provide overview variable names, labels, classes, representative values sortable, searchable table. output displayed interactive DT::datatable() Viewer pane (example RStudio Positron), allowing searching, sorting, export (copy, print, CSV, Excel, PDF) directly.

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
code: code_book(   x,   ...,   values = FALSE,   include_na = FALSE,   title = "Codebook",   filename = NULL,   factor_levels = c("all", "observed") )

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: x data frame tibble. ... Optional tidyselect-style column selectors (e.g. starts_with("var"), (.numeric), etc.). Columns can selected reordered, renaming selections supported. values Logical. FALSE (default), displays compact summary variable's values. numeric, character, date/time, labelled, factor variables, unique non-missing values shown four; otherwise first three values, ellipsis (...), last value shown. Values sorted appropriate (e.g., numeric, character, date). factors, factor_levels controls whether observed declared levels shown; level order preserved. labelled variables, prefixed labels displayed via labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed"). TRUE, unique non-missing values displayed. include_na Logical. TRUE, unique missing value markers (<NA>, <NaN>) explicitly appended end Values summary present variable. applies variable types. Literal strings "NA", "NaN", "" quoted distinguish missing markers. FALSE (default), missing values omitted Values still counted NAs column. title Optional character string displayed table caption. Defaults "Codebook". Set NULL remove title completely. filename = NULL, title also used base export filenames conversion portable ASCII name. filename Optional character string used base exported CSV, Excel, PDF filenames. NULL (default), portable filename derived title, falling back "Codebook" needed. File extensions added browser/export engine. factor_levels Character. Controls factor values displayed Values. "" (default; varlist() uses "observed") shows declared levels, including unused levels. "observed" shows levels present data, preserving factor level order.

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: DT::datatable object.

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: interactive datatable supports column sorting, global searching, client-side export (copy, print, CSV, Excel, PDF) directly Viewer. Variable selection uses tidyselect interface varlist(); underlying summary tibble built varlist() tbl = TRUE.

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: Requires following package: DT

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ if (requireNamespace("DT", quietly = TRUE)) {   code_book(sochealth)   code_book(sochealth, starts_with("bmi"))   code_book(sochealth, starts_with("bmi"), values = TRUE, include_na = TRUE)    factors <- data.frame(     group = factor(c("A", "B", NA), levels = c("A", "B", "C"))   )   code_book(     factors,     values = TRUE,     include_na = TRUE,     factor_levels = "observed"   )    code_book(     sochealth,     starts_with("bmi"),     title = "BMI codebook",     filename = "bmi_codebook"   ) } } # }

title: Pearson's contingency coefficient — contingency_coef
text: contingency_coef() computes Pearson's contingency coefficient C two-way contingency table.

title: Pearson's contingency coefficient — contingency_coef
code: contingency_coef(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Pearson's contingency coefficient — contingency_coef
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Pearson's contingency coefficient — contingency_coef
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test). CI values NA standard asymptotic SE exists C.

title: Pearson's contingency coefficient — contingency_coef
text: contingency coefficient \(C = \sqrt{\chi^2 / (\chi^2 + n)}\). ranges 0 (independence) maximum depends table dimensions. standard asymptotic standard error exists, confidence interval computed.

title: Pearson's contingency coefficient — contingency_coef
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) contingency_coef(tab) #> [1] 0.1344361

what: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
title: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
text: Copies data.frame, matrix, 2D higher array, table, atomic vector system clipboard, ready paste text editor, spreadsheet, word processor. Wraps clipr::write_clip() (Suggests dependency); requires clipr installed clipboard backend available platform.

title: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
code: copy_clipboard(   x,   row.names.as.col = FALSE,   row.names = TRUE,   col.names = TRUE,   show_message = TRUE,   quiet = FALSE,   ... )

title: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
text: x data.frame, matrix, 2D array, 3D array, table, atomic vector copied. row.names..col Logical character. FALSE (default), row names added column. TRUE, column named "rownames" prepended. character string, used column name promoted row names. Ignored (warning) x neither data.frame strict matrix. row.names Logical. TRUE (default), row names included clipboard output; FALSE omits . col.names Logical. TRUE (default), column names included clipboard output; FALSE omits . show_message Logical. TRUE (default), prints success message copying. quiet Logical. FALSE (default), messages shown. TRUE, suppresses messages, including success message, coercion notices, warnings. ... Additional arguments passed clipr::write_clip().

title: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
text: Invisibly returns x; function called clipboard side effect.

title: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
text: Objects data.frames 2D matrices (atomic vectors, arrays, tables) automatically coerced character way clipboard, required clipr::write_clip(). R-side object passed x never mutated. Multidimensional arrays (3D higher) flattened 1D character vector one element per line. preserve tabular layout, extract 2D slice first, e.g. copy_clipboard(my_array[, , 1]).

title: Copy data to the clipboard — copy_clipboard
code: # \donttest{ if (clipr::clipr_available()) {   # Data frame   copy_clipboard(sochealth)    # Data frame with row names as column   copy_clipboard(head(sochealth), row.names.as.col = "id")    # Matrix   mat <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2)   copy_clipboard(mat)    # Table   tbl <- table(sochealth$education)   copy_clipboard(tbl)    # Array (3D) -- flattened to character   arr <- array(1:8, dim = c(2, 2, 2))   copy_clipboard(arr)    # Recommended: copy 2D slice for tabular layout   copy_clipboard(arr[, , 1])    # Numeric vector   copy_clipboard(c(3.14, 2.71, 1.618))    # Character vector   copy_clipboard(c("apple", "banana", "cherry"))    # Quiet mode (no messages shown)   copy_clipboard(sochealth, quiet = TRUE) } # }

what: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
text: Counts, row data.frame matrix, many times one values appear across selected columns. Supports type-safe comparison (allow_coercion = FALSE), case-insensitive string matching (ignore_case = TRUE), detection special values (NA, NaN, Inf, -Inf) via special. Designed flow inside dplyr::mutate() pipelines.

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
code: count_n(   data = NULL,   select = tidyselect::everything(),   exclude = NULL,   count = NULL,   special = NULL,   allow_coercion = TRUE,   ignore_case = FALSE,   regex = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
text: data data.frame matrix. Optional inside dplyr::mutate(), current data context used automatically. select Columns include. Defaults tidyselect::everything(). Uses tidyselect helpers like tidyselect::starts_with(), etc. regex = TRUE, select treated regex string. exclude Character vector column names exclude selection. Defaults NULL (exclusion). count Value(s) count. Defaults NULL. Ignored special used. Multiple values allowed (e.g., count = c(1, 2, 3) count = c("yes", "")). R automatically coerces values count common type (e.g., c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2")), values expected final type. allow_coercion = FALSE, matching type-safe using identical(), type count must match values data. special Character vector special values count: "NA", "NaN", "Inf", "-Inf", "". Defaults NULL. "NA" uses .na(), therefore includes NA NaN values. "NaN" uses .nan() match actual NaN values. allow_coercion Logical. TRUE (default), values compared coercion. FALSE, uses strict matching via identical(). ignore_case Logical. FALSE (default), comparisons case-sensitive. TRUE, performs case-insensitive string comparisons. regex Logical. FALSE (default), uses tidyselect helpers. TRUE, interprets select regular expression pattern. verbose Logical. FALSE (default), messages suppressed. TRUE, prints processing messages.

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
text: numeric vector row-wise counts (unnamed), length nrow(data).

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
text: Comparison falls back identical() types differ, also inspects factor levels. Two consequences: count = "b" match factor "b" value: pass factor, e.g. count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)). Even factor count, comparisons columns whose level set differs return 0. guarantee perfect match (label levels), reuse value taken data (e.g. df$x[2]).

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
text: values converted lowercase via tolower() matching; factor columns first coerced character. mode takes precedence allow_coercion: equality becomes lowercase string equality, "b" "B" match even allow_coercion = FALSE.

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
text: R coerces mixed-type vectors construction time: count = c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2") function ever sees . get type-sensitive matching, keep count homogeneous.

title: Row-wise count of specific or special values — count_n
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(tibble) library(labelled)  # Basic usage df <- tibble(   x = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   y = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   z = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2") ) count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, count = 2, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 df |> mutate(num_twos = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>       x     y z     num_twos #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1     1     2 2            2 #> 2     2     2 2            3 #> 3     2    NA 2            2 #> 4     3     3 3            0 #> 5    NA     2 2            2  # Mixed types and special values df <- tibble(   num   = c(1, 2, NA, -Inf, NaN),   char  = c("a", "B", "b", "a", NA),   fact  = factor(c("a", "b", "b", "a", "c")),   date  = as.Date(c("2023-01-01", "2023-01-01", NA, "2023-01-02", "2023-01-01")),   lab   = labelled(c(1, 2, 1, 2, NA), labels = c(No = 1, Yes = 2)),   logic = c(TRUE, FALSE, NA, TRUE, FALSE) ) count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 0 2 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 0 2 3 0 0 count_n(df, count = "a", select = fact) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = as.Date("2023-01-01"), select = date) #> [1] 1 1 0 0 1  # Count special values count_n(df, special = "NA") #> [1] 0 0 3 0 3  # Column selection strategies df <- tibble(   score_math    = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   score_science = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   score_lang    = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2"),   name          = c("Jean", "Marie", "Ali", "Zoe", "Nina") ) count_n(df, select = c(score_math, score_science), count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = starts_with("score_"), exclude = "score_lang", count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = "^score_", regex = TRUE, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 df |> mutate(nb_two = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   score_math score_science score_lang name  nb_two #>        <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      <chr>  <dbl> #> 1          1             2 2          Jean       2 #> 2          2             2 2          Marie      3 #> 3          2            NA 2          Ali        2 #> 4          3             3 3          Zoe        0 #> 5         NA             2 2          Nina       2  # Strict type-safe matching with factor columns df <- tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) )  # Coercion: character "b" matches both x and y count_n(df, count = "b") #> [1] 1 1 0  # Strict match: fails because "b" is character, not factor (returns only 0s) count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Strict match with factor value: works only where levels match count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: cramer_v() computes Cramer's V two-way contingency table, measuring strength association two categorical variables.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
code: cramer_v(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: detail = FALSE: single numeric value (estimate). detail = TRUE conf_level non-NULL: c(estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value). detail = TRUE conf_level = NULL: c(estimate, p_value). p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Cramer's V computed \(V = \sqrt{\chi^2 / (n \cdot (k - 1))}\), \(\chi^2\) Pearson chi-squared statistic, \(n\) total count, \(k = \min(r, c)\). point estimate matches DescTools (Signorell et al., 2024) SPSS implementations. confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation \(V\) (\(\tanh(\mathrm{atanh}(V) \pm z_{\alpha/2} / \sqrt{n - 3})\)), differs noncentral chi-squared bootstrap CIs reported DescTools::CramerV().

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Agresti, . (2002). Categorical Data Analysis (2nd ed.). Wiley. Liebetrau, . M. (1983). Measures Association. Sage. Signorell, . et al. (2024). DescTools: Tools Descriptive Statistics. R package.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) cramer_v(tab) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tab, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  <.001 cramer_v(tab, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #> Estimate      p #>    0.136  <.001

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: Computes two-way cross-tabulation optional weights, grouping (including combinations multiple variables via interaction()), row / column percentages, inferential statistics (Chi-squared test APA-style association measure). x y required; one-way frequency tables, use freq().

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
code: cross_tab(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   by = NULL,   weights = NULL,   rescale = FALSE,   percent = c("none", "column", "row"),   include_stats = TRUE,   assoc_measure = c("auto", "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d",     "lambda", "none"),   assoc_ci = FALSE,   correct = FALSE,   simulate_p = FALSE,   simulate_B = 2000,   digits = NULL,   styled = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE,   decimal_mark = ".",   p_digits = 3L )

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: data data frame. Alternatively, vector using vector-based interface. x Row variable (unquoted). y Column variable (unquoted). Required; NULL default signature placeholder triggers error left unset (use freq() one-way tables). Optional grouping variable expression. Can single variable combination multiple variables (e.g. interaction(vs, )). weights Optional numeric weights. rescale Logical. FALSE (default), weights used -. TRUE, rescales weights total weighted N matches raw N. percent One "none" (default), "column", "row". Unique abbreviations accepted (e.g. "n", "c", "r"). include_stats Logical. TRUE (default), computes Chi-squared association measure (see assoc_measure). assoc_measure Character. association measure report. "auto" (default) selects Kendall's Tau-b variables ordered factors Cramer's V otherwise. choices: "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d", "lambda", "none". assoc_ci Logical. TRUE, includes 95 percent confidence interval association measure note. Defaults FALSE. correct Logical. FALSE (default), continuity correction applied. TRUE, applies Yates correction (2x2 tables). simulate_p Logical. FALSE (default), uses asymptotic p-values. TRUE, uses Monte Carlo simulation. simulate_B Integer. Number replicates Monte Carlo simulation. Defaults 2000. digits Number decimals cell values. Defaults NULL, resolved 1 percent != "none" 0 percent = "none" (counts always integers). styled Logical. TRUE (default), returns spicy_cross_table object (formatted printing). FALSE, returns plain data.frame. show_n Logical. TRUE (default), adds marginal N totals percent != "none". decimal_mark Character used decimal mark printed numeric values (cells, chi-squared, association estimate, CI bounds, p-value). Defaults ".". Set "," European formatting; matches decimal_mark argument table_*() family. p_digits Integer number decimals used format p-value (determine small-p threshold < .001 notation used). Defaults 3 (APA standard); matches p_digits argument table_*() family.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: Depends styled : styled = TRUE, : spicy_cross_table object (data.frame carrying rendering metadata attributes: title, digits, decimal_mark, n_row_idx, n_col_name, inferential block include_stats = TRUE). Printing dispatches print.spicy_cross_table(). styled = TRUE, supplied: spicy_cross_table_list, .e. named list spicy_cross_table objects (one element per group level, named level). Printing dispatches print.spicy_cross_table_list() renders table turn separated blank line. styled = FALSE: payload returned plain data.frame (named list data.frames ), stripped spicy_* classes downstream programmatic use. Cell columns levels y; rows levels x. percent != "none", N column (N row) added according show_n. include_stats = TRUE, result carries Chi-squared row (statistic, df, p) association-measure row (estimate, optional CI via assoc_ci).

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: function recognizes following global options modify default behavior: options(spicy.percent = "column") Sets default percentage mode calls cross_tab(). Valid values "none", "row", "column". Equivalent setting percent = "column" (another choice) call. options(spicy.simulate_p = TRUE) Enables Monte Carlo simulation Chi-squared tests default. Equivalent setting simulate_p = TRUE every call. options(spicy.rescale = TRUE) Automatically rescales weights total weighted N equals raw N. Equivalent setting rescale = TRUE call. options convenient users wish enforce consistent behavior across multiple calls cross_tab() spicy table functions. can disabled reset setting NULL: options(spicy.percent = NULL, spicy.simulate_p = NULL, spicy.rescale = NULL). Example:
code: options(spicy.simulate_p = TRUE, spicy.rescale = TRUE) cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight)

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: Creates frequency table vector variable data frame, options weighting, sorting, handling labelled data, defining custom missing values, displaying cumulative percentages. styled = TRUE, function prints spicy-formatted ASCII table using print.spicy_freq_table() spicy_print_table(); otherwise, returns data.frame containing frequencies proportions.

title: Frequency Table — freq
code: freq(   data,   x = NULL,   weights = NULL,   digits = 1L,   valid = TRUE,   cum = FALSE,   sort = "",   na_val = NULL,   labelled_levels = c("prefixed", "labels", "values"),   factor_levels = c("observed", "all"),   rescale = TRUE,   decimal_mark = ".",   styled = TRUE,   ... )

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: data data.frame, vector, factor. data frame provided, specify target variable x. data x supplied vectors, data ignored warning. x variable data (unquoted). weights Optional numeric vector weights (length x). variable may referenced bare name belongs data, qualified expression like $w (evaluated calling environment), always takes precedence data lookup. Observations NA weights dropped table warning; see Details. digits Number decimal digits display percentages (default: 1). valid Logical. TRUE (default), display valid percentages (excluding missing values). cum Logical. FALSE (default), cumulative percentages omitted. TRUE, adds cumulative percentages. sort Sorting method values: "" - sorting (default) "+" - increasing frequency "-" - decreasing frequency "name+" - alphabetical -Z "name-" - alphabetical Z-na_val Atomic vector numeric character values treated missing (NA). labelled variables (haven labelled), argument must refer underlying coded values, visible labels. Example:   labelled_levels labelled variables, defines labels values displayed: "prefixed" "p" - show labels [value] label (default) "labels" "l" - show labels "values" "v" - show numeric codes factor_levels Character. Controls factor labelled values displayed frequency table. "observed" (default; matches Stata's tab) shows levels present data. "" (matches SPSS FREQUENCIES code_book()'s default) keeps every declared level, including unused ones, appear n = 0. rescale Logical. TRUE (default), rescale weights total equals unweighted sample size (length(weights)). See Details interaction NA weights. decimal_mark Character used decimal mark printed percentages. Either "." (default) ",". Matches decimal_mark argument cross_tab() three table_*() helpers, European-locale users get consistent experience across package. styled Logical. TRUE (default), print formatted spicy table. FALSE, return plain data.frame frequency values. ... Additional arguments passed print.spicy_freq_table().
code: x <- labelled(c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3), c("Low" = 1, "Medium" = 2, "High" = 3)) freq(x, na_val = 1) # Treat all "Low" as missing

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: styled = FALSE, plain data.frame extra attributes columns: value - unique values factor levels n - frequency count (weighted applicable) prop - proportion total valid_prop - proportion valid responses (valid = TRUE) cum_prop, cum_valid_prop - cumulative percentages (cum = TRUE) styled = TRUE (default), prints formatted table console invisibly returns spicy_freq_table object: data.frame carrying rendering metadata attributes (digits, data_name, var_name, var_label, class_name, n_total, n_valid, weighted, rescaled, weight_var) used print.spicy_freq_table().

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: Designed mimic common frequency procedures SPSS Stata integrating flexibility R's data structures. input type (vector, factor, labelled) auto-detected; see @param labelled_levels @param factor_levels schema-vs-observed level controls, @param na_val optional sentinel-value recoding. Weighting (weights): frequencies percentages computed proportionally weights. Missing values weights cause observations dropped table entirely (warning), matching behaviour cross_tab() spicy 0.11.0+. rescale = TRUE, remaining (non-NA-weighted) weights normalised total weighted N equals count non-NA-weighted rows. rescale = FALSE, total weighted N actual sum non-NA weights. schema-level inspection without computing frequencies, use varlist() code_book().

title: Frequency Table — freq
code: # Frequency table with labelled ordered factor freq(sochealth, education) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        261       21.8  #>             │ Upper secondary        539       44.9  #>             │ Tertiary               400       33.3  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth freq(sochealth, self_rated_health, sort = "-") #> Frequency table: self_rated_health #>  #>  Category   │ Values         Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Good             558       46.5             47.3  #>             │ Very good        295       24.6             25.0  #>             │ Fair             266       22.2             22.5  #>             │ Poor              61        5.1              5.2  #>  Missing    │ NA                20        1.7                   #> ────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 1200      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Label: Self-rated health #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth  library(labelled)  # Simple numeric vector x <- c(1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, NA) freq(x) #> Frequency table: x #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ 1               1       14.3             16.7  #>             │ 2               2       28.6             33.3  #>             │ 3               3       42.9             50.0  #>  Missing    │ NA              1       14.3                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 7      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: numeric #> Data: x  # Plain vector with a sentinel value recoded as missing freq(c(1, 2, 3, 99, 99), na_val = 99) #> Frequency table: c(1, 2, 3, 99, 99) #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ 1               1       20.0             33.3  #>             │ 2               1       20.0             33.3  #>             │ 3               1       20.0             33.3  #>  Missing    │ NA              2       40.0                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 5      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: numeric #> Data: c(1, 2, 3, 99, 99)  # Labelled variable (haven-style) x_lbl <- labelled(   c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, NA),   labels = c("Low" = 1, "Medium" = 2, "High" = 3) ) var_label(x_lbl) <- "Satisfaction level"  # Treat value 1 ("Low") as missing freq(x_lbl, na_val = 1) #> Frequency table: x_lbl #>  #>  Category   │ Values          Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ [2] Medium          3       33.3             60.0  #>             │ [3] High            2       22.2             40.0  #>  Missing    │ NA                  4       44.4                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                     9      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Label: Satisfaction level #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, double #> Data: x_lbl  # Display only labels, add cumulative % freq(x_lbl, labelled_levels = "labels", cum = TRUE) #> Frequency table: x_lbl #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent    Cum. Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Low             3       33.3             37.5            33.3  #>             │ Medium          3       33.3             37.5            66.7  #>             │ High            2       22.2             25.0            88.9  #>  Missing    │ NA              1       11.1                            100.0  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 9      100.0            100.0           100.0  #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Cum. Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼──────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Low                       37.5  #>             │ Medium                    75.0  #>             │ High                     100.0  #>  Missing    │ NA                              #> ────────────┼──────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                          100.0  #>  #> Label: Satisfaction level #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, double #> Data: x_lbl  # Display values only, sorted descending freq(x_lbl, labelled_levels = "values", sort = "-") #> Frequency table: x_lbl #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ 1               3       33.3             37.5  #>             │ 2               3       33.3             37.5  #>             │ 3               2       22.2             25.0  #>  Missing    │ NA              1       11.1                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 9      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Label: Satisfaction level #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, double #> Data: x_lbl  # Show all declared factor levels, including unused ones (SPSS-style). # The default "observed" mirrors Stata's `tab` and drops unused levels. f <- factor(c("Yes", "No", "Yes"), levels = c("Yes", "No", "Maybe")) freq(f, factor_levels = "all") #> Frequency table: f #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Yes             2       66.7  #>             │ No              1       33.3  #>             │ Maybe           0        0.0  #> ────────────┼────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 3      100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: f  # With weighting df <- data.frame(   sex = factor(c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", NA, "Female")),   weight = c(12, 8, 10, 15, 7, 9) )  # Weighted frequencies (normalized) freq(df, sex, weights = weight, rescale = TRUE) #> Frequency table: sex #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Female          3       44.3             50.0  #>             │ Male            3       44.3             50.0  #>  Missing    │ NA              1       11.5                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 6      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df #> Weight: weight (rescaled)  # Weighted frequencies (without rescaling) freq(df, sex, weights = weight, rescale = FALSE) #> Frequency table: sex #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Female         27       44.3             50.0  #>             │ Male           27       44.3             50.0  #>  Missing    │ NA              7       11.5                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                61      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df #> Weight: weight  # Base R style, with weights and cumulative percentages freq(df$sex, weights = df$weight, cum = TRUE) #> Frequency table: sex #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent    Cum. Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Female          3       44.3             50.0            44.3  #>             │ Male            3       44.3             50.0            88.5  #>  Missing    │ NA              1       11.5                            100.0  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 6      100.0            100.0           100.0  #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Cum. Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼──────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Female                    50.0  #>             │ Male                     100.0  #>  Missing    │ NA                              #> ────────────┼──────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                          100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df #> Weight: df$weight (rescaled)  # Piped version (tidy syntax) and sort alphabetically descending ("name-") df |> freq(sex, sort = "name-") #> Frequency table: sex #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Male            2       33.3             40.0  #>             │ Female          3       50.0             60.0  #>  Missing    │ NA              1       16.7                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                 6      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: factor #> Data: df  # European decimal mark (matches `cross_tab()` and the `table_*()` family) freq(sochealth, education, decimal_mark = ",") #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        261       21,8  #>             │ Upper secondary        539       44,9  #>             │ Tertiary               400       33,3  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100,0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth  # Non-styled return (for programmatic use) f <- freq(df, sex, styled = FALSE) head(f) #>    value n      prop valid_prop #> 1 Female 3 0.5000000        0.6 #> 2   Male 2 0.3333333        0.4 #> 3   <NA> 1 0.1666667         NA

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: gamma_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal Gamma statistic two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: gamma_gk(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: gamma = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: Gamma computed \(\gamma = (C - D) / (C + D)\), \(C\) \(D\) numbers concordant discordant pairs. ignores tied pairs, making appropriate ordinal variables many ties. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) gamma_gk(tab) #> [1] 0.3104791 gamma_gk(tab, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  <.001

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: goodman_kruskal_tau() computes Goodman-Kruskal's Tau, proportional reduction error (PRE) measure nominal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(   x,   direction = c("row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row) "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: Unlike lambda_gk(), Goodman-Kruskal's Tau uses cell frequencies rather modal categories, making sensitive association patterns lambda may zero. Goodman-Kruskal's Tau intrinsically directional canonical symmetric form (unlike lambda_gk() uncertainty_coef()); "row" "column" supported. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) goodman_kruskal_tau(tab) #> [1] 0.01840572 goodman_kruskal_tau(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.008     0.001     0.014  .022

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: kendall_tau_b() computes Kendall's Tau-b two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
code: kendall_tau_b(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: Kendall's Tau-b computed \(\tau_b = (C - D) / \sqrt{(n_0 - n_1)(n_0 - n_2)}\), \(n_0 = n(n-1)/2\), \(n_1\) number pairs tied row variable, \(n_2\) number tied column variable. Tau-b corrects ties appropriate square tables. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) kendall_tau_b(tab) #> [1] 0.2045524

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: kendall_tau_c() computes Stuart's Tau-c (also known Kendall's Tau-c) two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
code: kendall_tau_c(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: Stuart's Tau-c computed \(\tau_c = 2m(C - D) / (n^2(m - 1))\), \(m = \min(r, c)\). designed rectangular tables; estimate bounded \([-1, 1]\) table square, may fall outside range otherwise. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) kendall_tau_c(tab) #> [1] 0.1996409

what: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: Splits column name first occurrence sep, renames column part sep (name, trimmed surrounding whitespace), assigns part sep "label" attribute column. label attribute follows haven convention also used labelled::var_label(), labelled-aware tooling (labelled, haven, varlist(), code_book(), ...) reads transparently. Splitting first sep means label may contain separator.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: label_from_names(df, sep = ". ")

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: df data.frame tibble column names form "name<sep>label" (e.g. "code. question text"). sep Character string used separator name label. Default ". " (LimeSurvey's default); literal string can used. Matched fixed string, regex metacharacters . | carry special meaning.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: object class df – base data.frame df base data.frame, tbl_df df tibble. output column names equal trimmed names (sep) , every column whose original name contained sep, "label" attribute equal label (sep). Columns whose name contain sep passed unchanged label attached.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: Designed primarily LimeSurvey CSV exports Headings: Question code & question text, produce column names like "code. question text". default separator ". " matches export. LimeSurvey question codes (part sep) restricted alphanumerics, must start letter, contain spaces – column name carry code question text. export uses Headings: Question code (codes ), re-export Question code & question text calling function; way recover label code alone. Whitespace handling: name (left sep) trimmed surrounding whitespace, R column names intended referenced bare (without backticks) leading / trailing whitespace force quoting throughout user's downstream code. label (right sep) preserved verbatim, following Stata / SPSS convention variable labels faithful user content – spicy silently mutate label strings. trim labels , post-process labelled::var_label(df) <- lapply(labelled::var_label(df), trimws).

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: function raises actionable error – rather letting downstream constructor raise cryptic one – split produces: duplicate column names (two original names share prefix sep); empty column name (original name starts sep nothing ).

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: # LimeSurvey-style column names (default sep = ". "). df <- data.frame(   "age. Age of respondent" = c(25, 30),   "score. Total score. Manually computed." = c(12, 14),   check.names = FALSE ) out <- label_from_names(df) attr(out$age, "label") #> [1] "Age of respondent" attr(out$score, "label") #> [1] "Total score. Manually computed."  # Custom separator. df2 <- data.frame(   "id|Identifier" = 1:3,   "score|Total score" = c(10, 20, 30),   check.names = FALSE ) out2 <- label_from_names(df2, sep = "|")

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: lambda_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda, proportional reduction error (PRE) measure nominal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
code: lambda_gk(   x,   direction = c("symmetric", "row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "symmetric" (default), "row" (column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: Lambda measures much prediction error reduced independent variable used predict dependent variable. ranges 0 (reduction) 1 (perfect prediction). Lambda can equal zero even variables associated modal category dominates every column (row). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) lambda_gk(tab) #> [1] 0 lambda_gk(tab, direction = "row") #> [1] 0 lambda_gk(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper   p #>    0.000     0.000     0.000  --

what: Row means with an optional minimum-valid-values rule — mean_n
title: Row means with an optional minimum-valid-values rule — mean_n
text: Computes row-wise means across selected numeric columns data.frame matrix. Missing values handled per row via min_valid (integer count proportion non-NA values required); rows fail rule return NA. Non-numeric columns dropped silently (set verbose = TRUE see ). Designed flow inside dplyr::mutate(): called without explicit data argument, current data context used.

title: Row means with an optional minimum-valid-values rule — mean_n
code: mean_n(   data = NULL,   select = tidyselect::everything(),   exclude = NULL,   min_valid = NULL,   digits = NULL,   regex = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Row means with an optional minimum-valid-values rule — mean_n
text: data data.frame matrix. Optional inside dplyr::mutate(), current grouping/data context used automatically. select Columns include. regex = FALSE, use tidyselect syntax (default: tidyselect::everything()). regex = TRUE, provide regular expression pattern (character string). exclude Columns exclude (default: NULL). min_valid Minimum number valid (non-NA) values required per row. Accepts: NULL (default) – every selected column must valid. proportion (0, 1) – round(ncol(x) * min_valid) valid columns required (e.g. min_valid = 0.5 requires least half selected columns non-NA). non-negative integer count number selected numeric columns. Non-integer values >= 1 (e.g. 1.5) counts greater ncol(x) raise actionable error. digits Optional non-negative integer giving number decimal places round result . Defaults NULL (rounding). regex Logical. FALSE (default), uses tidyselect helpers. TRUE, select argument treated regular expression. verbose Logical. FALSE (default), messages suppressed. TRUE, prints message non-numeric columns excluded.

title: Row means with an optional minimum-valid-values rule — mean_n
text: numeric vector row-wise means.

title: Row means with an optional minimum-valid-values rule — mean_n
code: library(dplyr)  # Create a simple numeric data frame df <- tibble(   var1 = c(10, NA, 30, 40, 50),   var2 = c(5, NA, 15, NA, 25),   var3 = c(NA, 30, 20, 50, 10) )  # Compute row-wise mean (all values must be valid by default) mean_n(df) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Require at least 2 valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 2) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Require at least 50% valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 0.5) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Round the result to 1 decimal mean_n(df, digits = 1) #> [1]   NA   NA 21.7   NA 28.3  # Select specific columns mean_n(df, select = c(var1, var2)) #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select specific columns using a pipe df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mean_n() #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Exclude a column mean_n(df, exclude = "var3") #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select columns ending with "1" mean_n(df, select = ends_with("1")) #> [1] 10 NA 30 40 50  # Use with native pipe df |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use inside dplyr::mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Select columns directly inside mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = c(var1, var2), min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       22.5 #> 4    40    NA    50       40   #> 5    50    25    10       37.5  # Select columns before mutate df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>    var1  var2 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA       NA   #> 3    30    15       22.5 #> 4    40    NA       40   #> 5    50    25       37.5  # Show verbose processing info df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2, digits = 1, verbose = TRUE)) #> mean_n(): Row means computed with min_valid = 2, regex = FALSE #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Add character and grouping columns df_mixed <- mutate(df,   name = letters[1:5],   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "A") ) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A     #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A     #> 3    30    15    20 c     B     #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B     #> 5    50    25    10 e     A      # Non-numeric columns are ignored mean_n(df_mixed) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use within mutate() on mixed data df_mixed |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = starts_with("var"))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # Use everything() but exclude non-numeric columns manually mean_n(df_mixed, select = everything(), exclude = "group") #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Select columns using regex mean_n(df_mixed, select = "^var", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333 mean_n(df_mixed, select = "ar", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Apply to a subset of rows (first 3) df_mixed[1:3, ] |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667  # Store the result in a new column df_mixed$mean_score <- mean_n(df_mixed, select = starts_with("var")) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # With a numeric matrix mat <- matrix(c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5, NA, 7, 8, 9), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) mat #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    2   NA #> [2,]    4    5   NA #> [3,]    7    8    9 mat |> mean_n(min_valid = 2) #> [1] 1.5 4.5 8.0

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: phi() computes phi coefficient 2x2 contingency table.

title: Phi coefficient — phi
code: phi(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, digits = 3L, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: phi coefficient \(\phi = \sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\). equivalent Cramer's V 2x2 tables equals absolute value Pearson correlation two binary variables – spicy returns magnitude (always non-negative), matching DescTools (Signorell et al., 2024) SPSS conventions. recover signed direction 2x2 association, compute Pearson correlation directly (e.g. cor(x, y) coding variables 0/1). confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation \(\phi\); see cramer_v() formula full references.

title: Phi coefficient — phi
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$sex) phi(tab) #> [1] 0.0107495 phi(tab, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.011     0.000     0.068  .713

title: Simulated social-health survey — sochealth
text: simulated dataset 1200 respondents fictional social-health survey, designed illustrate main features spicy package: variable labels, ordered factors, survey weights, association measures, APA-style reporting.

title: Simulated social-health survey — sochealth
code: sochealth

title: Simulated social-health survey — sochealth
text: tibble 1200 rows 24 variables: sex Factor. Sex respondent. age Numeric. Age years (25–75). age_group Ordered factor. Age group (25–34, 35–49, 50–64, 65–75). education Ordered factor. Highest education level (Lower secondary, Upper secondary, Tertiary). social_class Ordered factor. Subjective social class (Lower, Working, Lower middle, Middle, Upper middle). region Factor. Region residence (6 regions). employment_status Factor. Employment status (Employed, Student, Unemployed, Inactive). income_group Ordered factor. Household income group (Low, Lower middle, Upper middle, High). Contains missing values. income Numeric. Monthly household income CHF (1000–7400). smoking Factor. Current smoker (, Yes). Contains missing values. physical_activity Factor. Regular physical activity (, Yes). dentist_12m Factor. Dentist visit last 12 months (, Yes). self_rated_health Ordered factor. Self-rated health (Poor, Fair, Good, good). Contains missing values. wellbeing_score Numeric. -5 wellbeing index (0–100). bmi Numeric. Body mass index kg/m\(^2\) (16–39). Contains missing values. bmi_category Ordered factor. BMI category (Normal weight, Overweight, Obesity). Contains missing values. institutional_trust Ordered factor. Trust institutions (low, Low, High, high). political_position Numeric. Political position 0 (left) 10 (right) scale. Contains missing values. life_sat_health Integer. Satisfaction health (1–5 Likert scale). Contains missing values. life_sat_work Integer. Satisfaction work main activity (1–5 Likert scale). Contains missing values. life_sat_relationships Integer. Satisfaction personal relationships (1–5 Likert scale). Contains missing values. life_sat_standard Integer. Satisfaction standard living (1–5 Likert scale). Contains missing values. response_date POSIXct. Date time survey response (September–November 2024). weight Numeric. Survey design weight (range 0.29–3.45); calibrated sum(weight) matches unweighted N mean(weight) approximately 1. See Details.

title: Simulated social-health survey — sochealth
text: Simulated data illustration purposes; reproducible sourcing data-raw/sochealth.R. script seeds main generation block set.seed(2025), two missing-value injection blocks set.seed(2027) (four life_sat_* items) set.seed(2026) (smoking, self_rated_health, income_group, political_position, bmi).

title: Simulated social-health survey — sochealth
text: Every variable carries "label" attribute (read labelled::var_label() surfaced varlist() / code_book()). mix factor types deliberate: nominal factors (sex, region, ...) ordered factors (education, self_rated_health, ...) live side side cross_tab() table_categorical() can demonstrate automatic ordinal-vs-nominal dispatch (Cramer's V, Phi, Kendall's Tau-b, Goodman-Kruskal Gamma) dataset. Survey weights (weight) calibrated: sum(weight) matches unweighted N within rounding (\(\approx 1200\)) mean(weight) \(\approx 1\). Weighted means therefore agree unweighted means sampling noise without rescaling.

title: Simulated social-health survey — sochealth
code: data(sochealth) varlist(sochealth) #> Non-interactive session: use `tbl = TRUE` to return a tibble. freq(sochealth, education) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        261       21.8  #>             │ Upper secondary        539       44.9  #>             │ Tertiary               400       33.3  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth cross_tab(sochealth, education, self_rated_health) #> Crosstable: education x self_rated_health (N) #>  #>  Values            │   Poor    Fair    Good    Very good │   Total  #> ───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  Lower secondary   │     28      86     102           44 │     260  #>  Upper secondary   │     28     118     263          118 │     527  #>  Tertiary          │      5      62     193          133 │     393  #> ───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  Total             │     61     266     558          295 │    1180  #>  #> Chi-2(6) = 73.2, p <.001 #> Kendall's Tau-b = 0.20

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: somers_d() computes Somers' D two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
code: somers_d(   x,   direction = c("row", "column", "symmetric"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column), "symmetric" (average directions). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: Somers' D asymmetric ordinal measure defined \(d = (C - D) / (C + D + T)\), \(T\) number pairs tied independent variable. symmetric version (direction = "symmetric") harmonic mean two asymmetric values, matching SPSS / PSPP convention; identical Kendall's Tau-b (geometric mean two quantities), although two often agree two decimals. analytic SE / CI reported symmetric form (DescTools follows convention). Standard error formulas asymmetric directions follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) somers_d(tab, direction = "row") #> [1] 0.2015369 somers_d(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.208     0.157     0.258  <.001

what: spicy: descriptive statistics, summary tables, and data management — spicy-package
title: spicy: descriptive statistics, summary tables, and data management — spicy-package
text: spicy provides tools descriptive data analysis, variable inspection, tabulation workflows: frequency tables, cross-tabulations chi-squared tests effect sizes, association measures contingency tables, categorical continuous summary tables, model-based linear-regression tables optional additive covariate adjustment, row-wise descriptive summaries, interactive codebooks, variable-label extraction, clipboard export.

title: spicy: descriptive statistics, summary tables, and data management — spicy-package
text: spicy active pre-1.0 development. Breaking changes made deliberately minor-version bumps always announced NEWS.md. API surface partitioned follows; users planning embed spicy production pipelines downstream packages rely stable surface. Stable (signature behaviour preserved across 0.y.z 1.0.0; documented changes ): Frequency / cross-tabs: freq(), cross_tab() Variable inspection: varlist() / vl(), code_book(), label_from_names() Row-wise summaries: mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n() Clipboard export: copy_clipboard() Association measures (point estimates documented CIs): cramer_v(), phi(), contingency_coef(), yule_q(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), kendall_tau_c(), somers_d(), lambda_gk(), goodman_kruskal_tau(), uncertainty_coef() Stabilising (still maturing; argument names may tightened 1.0 NEWS.md entry, silent behavioural changes): Summary table builders: table_categorical(), table_continuous(), table_continuous_lm() Omnibus association overview: assoc_measures() Internal API (part public surface; can change without notice – avoid calling directly downstream code): ASCII rendering primitives: build_ascii_table(), spicy_print_table()

title: spicy: descriptive statistics, summary tables, and data management — spicy-package
text: broom::tidy() broom::glance() methods spicy_freq_table, spicy_categorical_table, spicy_continuous_table, spicy_continuous_lm_table follow standard broom column conventions (outcome, term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, statistic, p.value, df, df.residual, r.squared, adj.r.squared, nobs, ...). set columns produced method considered stabilising: existing columns silently renamed semantics changed within 0.y.z, breaking change announced NEWS.md. Adding optional new columns (e.g. covariate-adjustment metadata) breaking change. Numeric columns always returned type downstream broom-consumers expect: df integer, df.residual numeric (Satterthwaite-corrected degrees freedom cluster-robust variance modes preserved verbatim, matching lmerTest::glance() afex output convention).

title: spicy: descriptive statistics, summary tables, and data management — spicy-package
text: errors warnings emitted stable / stabilising surfaces carry classed conditions downstream code can dispatch class via tryCatch() / withCallingHandlers() instead matching message strings. condition package-wide parent class plus leaf class describing specific cause: spicy_error Catch-parent every error raised spicy. Leaves: spicy_invalid_input – bad argument value type. spicy_invalid_data – bad data shape (data.frame, NA cells forbidden, length mismatch). spicy_missing_pkg – Suggests dependency required requested operation installed. spicy_missing_column – referenced column data. spicy_unsupported – operation applicable input (e.g., Phi requested non-2x2 table). spicy_warning Catch-parent every warning. Leaves: spicy_undefined_stat – requested statistic undefined input; result NA (e.g., Tau-b table -zero marginals). spicy_dropped_na – NA observations silently excluded computation (e.g., NA weights). spicy_ignored_arg – argument ignored due context (e.g., correct = TRUE non-2x2 table). spicy_no_selection – column selector produced empty set; empty result returned rather erroring. spicy_fallback – requested computation failed; simpler estimator used instead. spicy_summary_failed – varlist() summarise one column; rest table fine. spicy_renamed_column – user data column factor level collided spicy-internal name auto-renamed preserve data (emitted cross_tab()).
